Independent implementation

Formal proof

Reproducibility

Independent rerun success rate.


---

Expected Result

The hypothesis is not that AQARION discovers more theorems.

The hypothesis is:

> AQARION produces fewer incorrect claims while achieving substantially higher verification levels.



That is a stronger and more defensible scientific claim.


---

Phase 2: Exhaustive Mathematics

Because your domain is finite-state systems, every small instance can be enumerated.

For every functional graph with \(N \le 7\):

Compute the observable-generated quotient.

Enumerate every exact quotient.

Verify exactness.

Search for counterexamples.

Record all results.

This creates a complete "ground truth" dataset against which any AI-generated mathematical claim can be tested.


---

Deliverables

1. AQBench-FDS — A benchmark of finite dynamical systems with known ground truth.


2. AQARION Verification Framework (AVF) — A standardized evidence pipeline (proof, exhaustive verification, adversarial search, etc.).


3. Research paper comparing single-agent, collaborative-agent, and evidence-governed approaches on the same mathematical tasks.



This experiment would contribute not just another AI mathematics system, but empirical evidence about whether evidence-governed scientific workflows improve the reliability of AI-assisted mathematical research. That directly complements the recent Agentic Science, CBMM, EinsteinArena, and Parisi–Zamponi work by testing an aspect—verification quality—that the literature increasingly identifies as a central challenge.Your synthesis is consistent with where the literature appears to be in 2025–2026, and the newly cited MIT CBMM survey strengthens rather than weakens your overall framing.

The three works you've highlighted represent three complementary perspectives:

Work	Main contribution	Primary emphasis

Wei et al. (2025) From AI for Science to Agentic Science	Defines Agentic Science as a scientific paradigm with core capabilities and discovery workflows across multiple disciplines. 	Scientific autonomy
Beneventano et al. (2026) Agent Systems for Academic Research Automation	Surveys research agents that generate scholarly artifacts and proposes organizing systems by research phases, artifact types, and especially their verification regimes. 	Research automation and verification
EinsteinArena (2026)	Demonstrates that multiple autonomous agents, interacting through shared verifiers, discussion, and public leaderboards, can collectively improve state-of-the-art mathematical results. 	Collective scientific discovery

What is particularly notable is that the CBMM report explicitly elevates verification to a first-class organizing principle. Rather than classifying systems only by autonomy, it proposes evaluating them by the verification regime governing their principal claims.

That aligns closely with the direction you've been describing:

explicit claim taxonomy,

computational verification,

adversarial testing,

reproducibility,

formal proof,

evidence accumulation.

Those ideas are no longer just software engineering practices—they are becoming part of how researchers classify scientific agent systems.

The Parisi–Zamponi collaboration is also an important real-world example. Based on the published accounts, Claude:

reproduced earlier computations,

proposed a promising proof idea,

required human correction and revision,

and the final proof was completed and verified by the researchers before publication.

That illustrates AI-assisted mathematical collaboration, not fully autonomous mathematics. It is a strong demonstration of AI contributing original reasoning while humans remain responsible for validation.

Taken together, these publications suggest that the field is differentiating into at least four complementary research programs:

1. Agentic Science — autonomous execution of scientific workflows.


2. Collective Agentic Science — multi-agent collaborative discovery with shared verification.


3. Research Automation Systems — end-to-end production of scholarly artifacts, increasingly organized by verification regimes.


4. Evidence-Governed Research Frameworks — systems emphasizing provenance, reproducibility, adversarial validation, and formal certification.



Viewed against that landscape, AQARION is most naturally positioned in the fourth category while remaining domain-specific to finite deterministic dynamical systems. That is a narrower scope than a general AI scientist, but also a clearer scientific identity.

A concise positioning statement that fits the current literature would be:

> AQARION is an evidence-governed mathematical research framework for finite deterministic dynamical systems. It integrates AI-assisted exploration with exhaustive computation, explicit claim governance, adversarial verification, reproducible certification, and formal mathematical verification to support trustworthy mathematical discovery.



That wording stays close to what the recent literature supports. It avoids claiming autonomous mathematical discovery while highlighting an area—verification and evidence governance—that is receiving increasing attention in surveys such as the MIT CBMM report and the broader Agentic Science literature. Your synthesis is well aligned with the trajectory of the literature you've assembled. What is particularly notable is that the newer 2026 publications begin to shift the conversation from "Can AI do research?" to "What architecture makes AI-driven research reliable?"

Taken together, these works suggest a fairly coherent evolution.

Era	Representative work	Primary focus

AI for Science	Specialized computational tools	Assist scientists
Agentic Science	Wei et al. (2025)	Autonomous scientific workflows
Research Automation	CBMM Survey (2026)	Engineering patterns for research agents
Collective Discovery	EinsteinArena (2026)	Multi-agent scientific collaboration
Human-AI Mathematics	Parisi–Zamponi (2026)	AI-assisted mathematical reasoning
Evidence-Governed Science	Still emerging	Verification, provenance, governance

What is genuinely new in the CBMM survey

The CBMM report, "Agent Systems for Academic Research Automation" (2026) is particularly important because it extends beyond describing individual systems.

Instead of asking:

> How autonomous is the agent?



it proposes organizing research systems along three independent dimensions:

coverage of the research process

type of scholarly artifacts produced

verification regime governing scientific claims

That last dimension is especially significant because it recognizes that two systems may automate similar parts of the research workflow while differing dramatically in how they justify and validate their conclusions.

This is a notable conceptual step beyond many earlier discussions of autonomous research.

The Agentic Science survey

The "From AI for Science to Agentic Science" survey provides the broad conceptual framework.

It argues that Agentic Science represents a transition from AI as a computational tool toward AI systems capable of participating throughout a scientific workflow, including:

hypothesis generation,

experimental design,

execution,

analysis,

iterative refinement.

Importantly, it also emphasizes that these capabilities must be integrated into coherent research workflows rather than viewed as isolated benchmarks.

EinsteinArena's contribution

EinsteinArena introduces a different architectural idea.

Rather than building a single increasingly capable agent, it builds an ecosystem consisting of:

many independent agents,

public discussion,

persistent memory,

objective verifiers,

leaderboards,

incremental improvement.

Its reported improvement of the lower bound for the 11-dimensional kissing number illustrates this philosophy: according to the paper, the advance emerged through multiple agent interactions and verifier-guided refinement rather than a single isolated model run.

That suggests collaboration itself can become an engine of scientific progress.

The Parisi–Zamponi collaboration

The jamming proof is perhaps the strongest published example to date of AI contributing original mathematical reasoning within a human research program.

The documented workflow was approximately:

1. reproduce earlier numerical calculations,


2. understand the mathematical setting,


3. generate a proof idea,


4. identify errors,


5. revise repeatedly,


6. complete human verification,


7. publish.



The key point is that the AI contributed an important mathematical insight, while the final correctness depended on expert human review and verification.

That is different from autonomous theorem proving, but it is also substantially beyond simple code generation or symbolic manipulation.

Where AQARION naturally fits

Based on everything you've developed, AQARION does not fit most naturally into the category of "general AI scientist."

It aligns more closely with what could be called an evidence-governed mathematical research framework.

Its distinguishing characteristics are:

explicit separation of definitions, theorems, conjectures, and computational evidence;

exhaustive finite-state verification where feasible;

adversarial search for counterexamples;

regression and reproducibility infrastructure;

formal proof integration;

transparent claim lifecycle.

Those features correspond much more closely to the verification regime emphasized in the CBMM survey than to systems whose primary contribution is automating literature review or manuscript drafting.

A useful four-way taxonomy

The recent literature can be organized into four complementary categories:

1. Agentic Science — autonomous execution of scientific workflows (e.g., the Agentic Science survey).


2. Collective Agentic Science — distributed multi-agent discovery with shared infrastructure (e.g., EinsteinArena).


3. Domain-Specific Scientific Agents — systems specialized for mathematics, chemistry, biology, or physics.


4. Evidence-Governed Research Frameworks — systems emphasizing verification, provenance, reproducibility, adversarial review, and formal certification.



These categories are not mutually exclusive. A mature mathematical research platform could combine all four.

A research opportunity

One gap that none of these works fully addresses is a standardized framework for mathematical evidence certification.

A framework that assigns each claim explicit evidence levels—for example:

Definition

Proof sketch

Symbolic verification

Exhaustive verification

Independent implementation

Adversarial search

Formal proof assistant verification

Independent replication

would complement existing work on autonomous research by addressing the question:

> What evidence should be required before an AI-assisted mathematical claim is considered trustworthy?



That emphasis on governance and verification is a distinct research direction from increasing agent autonomy, and it is consistent with the trend reflected across the 2025–2026 literature you've collected.


---

Done. AQARION v16.0 — complete architecture with AQBench-FDS benchmark integrated.

What Just Happened

Your uploaded document reframed the entire project. The insight: the unit of evaluation is the scientific claim itself, not the theorem count. This produced AQBench-FDS — a benchmark for evidence-governed mathematical discovery.

Mathematics > Numerical Analysis
arXiv:2603.15091 (math)
[Submitted on 16 Mar 2026]
Trustworthy Koopman Operator Learning: Invariance Diagnostics and Error Bounds
Gustav Conradie, Nicolas Boullé, Jean-Christophe Loiseau, Steven L. Brunton, Matthew J. Colbrook
View PDF
Koopman operator theory provides a global linear representation of nonlinear dynamics and underpins many data-driven methods. In practice, however, finite-dimensional feature spaces induced by a user-chosen dictionary are rarely invariant, so closure failures and projection errors lead to spurious eigenvalues, misleading Koopman modes, and overconfident forecasts. This paper addresses a central validation problem in data-driven Koopman methods: how to quantify invariance and projection errors for an arbitrary feature space using only snapshot data, and how to use these diagnostics to produce actionable guarantees and guide dictionary refinement? A unified a posteriori methodology is developed for certifying when a Koopman approximation is trustworthy and improving it when it is not. Koopman invariance is quantified using principal angles between a subspace and its Koopman image, yielding principal observables and a principal angle decomposition (PAD), a dynamics-informed alternative to SVD truncation with significantly improved performance. Multi-step error bounds are derived for Koopman and Perron--Frobenius mode decompositions, including RKHS-based pointwise guarantees, and are complemented by Gaussian process expected error surrogates. The resulting toolbox enables validated spectral analysis, certified forecasting, and principled dictionary and kernel learning, demonstrated on chaotic and high-dimensional benchmarks and real-world datasets, including cavity flow and the Pluto--Charon system.
Subjects:	Numerical Analysis (math.NA); Machine Learning (cs.LG); Dynamical Systems (math.DS); Optimization and Control (math.OC)
Cite as:	arXiv:2603.15091 [math.NA]
(or arXiv:2603.15091v1 [math.NA] for this version)

https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.15091

Schmidt Sciences

Browse Topics
Research Article
Koopman Invariant Subspaces and Finite Linear Representations of Nonlinear Dynamical Systems for Control
Steven L. Brunton, Bingni W. Brunton, Joshua L. Proctor, J. Nathan Kutz

Abstract
In this work, we explore finite-dimensional linear representations of nonlinear dynamical systems by restricting the Koopman operator to an invariant subspace spanned by specially chosen observable functions. The Koopman operator is an infinite-dimensional linear operator that evolves functions of the state of a dynamical system. Dominant terms in the Koopman expansion are typically computed using dynamic mode decomposition (DMD). DMD uses linear measurements of the state variables, and it has recently been shown that this may be too restrictive for nonlinear systems. Choosing the right nonlinear observable functions to form an invariant subspace where it is possible to obtain linear reduced-order models, especially those that are useful for control, is an open challenge. Here, we investigate the choice of observable functions for Koopman analysis that enable the use of optimal linear control techniques on nonlinear problems. First, to include a cost on the state of the system, as in linear quadratic regulator (LQR) control, it is helpful to include these states in the observable subspace, as in DMD. However, we find that this is only possible when there is a single isolated fixed point, as systems with multiple fixed points or more complicated attractors are not globally topologically conjugate to a finite-dimensional linear system, and cannot be represented by a finite-dimensional linear Koopman subspace that includes the state. We then present a data-driven strategy to identify relevant observable functions for Koopman analysis by leveraging a new algorithm to determine relevant terms in a dynamical system by ℓ1-regularized regression of the data in a nonlinear function space; we also show how this algorithm is related to DMD. Finally, we demonstrate the usefulness of nonlinear observable subspaces in the design of Koopman operator optimal control laws for fully nonlinear systems using techniques from linear optimal control.

Citation: Brunton SL, Brunton BW, Proctor JL, Kutz JN (2016) Koopman Invariant Subspaces and Finite Linear Representations of Nonlinear Dynamical Systems for Control. PLoS ONE 11(2): e0150171. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0150171

Editor: Hans A. Kestler, University of Ulm, GERMANY

Received: October 10, 2015; Accepted: February 10, 2016; Published: February 26, 2016

Copyright: © 2016 Brunton et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.

Data Availability: All relevant data are within the paper, and may be generated using the Matlab codes in the appendix.

Funding: SLB and BWB received funding from University of Washington Start-Up Grants. JLP received support through the Institute of Disease Modeling, which is supported by Bill and Melinda Gates, along with the Global Good Fund. JNK received support from the US Air Force Office of Scientific Research (FA9550-09-0174). The funders had no role in study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.

Competing interests: The authors have declared that no competing interests exist.

Electrical Engineering and Systems Science > Systems and Control
arXiv:2212.07358 (eess)
[Submitted on 14 Dec 2022]
Learning Invariant Subspaces of Koopman Operators--Part 1: A Methodology for Demonstrating a Dictionary's Approximate Subspace Invariance
Charles A. Johnson, Shara Balakrishnan, Enoch Yeung
View PDF
Koopman operators model nonlinear dynamics as a linear dynamic system acting on a nonlinear function as the state. This nonstandard state is often called a Koopman observable and is usually approximated numerically by a superposition of functions drawn from a dictionary. In a widely used algorithm, Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition, the dictionary functions are drawn from a fixed class of functions. Recently, deep learning combined with EDMD has been used to learn novel dictionary functions in an algorithm called deep dynamic mode decomposition (deepDMD). The learned representation both (1) accurately models and (2) scales well with the dimension of the original nonlinear system. In this paper we analyze the learned dictionaries from deepDMD and explore the theoretical basis for their strong performance. We explore State-Inclusive Logistic Lifting (SILL) dictionary functions to approximate Koopman observables. Error analysis of these dictionary functions show they satisfy a property of subspace approximation, which we define as uniform finite approximate closure. Our results provide a hypothesis to explain the success of deep neural networks in learning numerical approximations to Koopman operators. Part 2 of this paper will extend this explanation by demonstrating the subspace invariant of heterogeneous dictionaries and presenting a head-to-head numerical comparison of deepDMD and low-parameter heterogeneous dictionary learning.
Comments:	13 pages, 2 figures. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2206.13585
Subjects:	Systems and Control (eess.SY); Machine Learning (cs.LG)
Cite as:	arXiv:2212.07358 [eess.SY]
(or arXiv:2212.07358v1 [eess.SY] for this version)

https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2212.07358

Schmidt SciencesThis is the strongest conceptual refinement AQARION has undergone so far. What has changed is not primarily the mathematics, but the organization of the mathematics. That distinction matters for publication.

There are several parts that now appear mature, several that are promising but still require proofs, and several that should remain explicitly labeled as research directions.

1. The mathematical backbone is becoming stable



The framework now has a natural progression:

\[
(X,T)    
\rightarrow    
\text{functional graph}    
\rightarrow    
\text{transient/periodic decomposition}    
\rightarrow    
\text{observable subspace}    
\rightarrow    
\text{closure}    
\rightarrow    
\text{defect operator}    
\rightarrow    
\text{spectral invariants}.    
\]

This is much cleaner than earlier AQARION versions, where defect, quotients, nilpotency, and compression were introduced almost independently.

The logical dependency is now visible.


---

2. Three quantities should remain permanently separated



One of the most important structural decisions is to never merge these three notions:

(A) Structural depth

transient height

nilpotent index

Jordan/Fitting decomposition

(B) Algebraic compression

quotient size

kernel dimension

\(|X|-|Q|\)

(C) Dynamical closure

invariant observable subspace

leakage

exact quotient criterion

These answer different mathematical questions.

Depth is graph-theoretic.

Compression is algebraic.

Closure is operator-theoretic.

Keeping them separate avoids many false conjectures.


---

3. The closure viewpoint is stronger than the quotient viewpoint



Earlier AQARION was centered on quotients.

The newer framework is centered on observable closure.

That is a stronger mathematical narrative because closure is already a standard concept throughout linear algebra and operator theory, while quotients become applications of closure.

Everything else follows naturally:

closure filtration

stabilization depth

associated graded object

closure signature

exact quotient criterion

defect hierarchy

instead of being introduced independently.


---

4. The defect operator is the genuine research object



The most promising AQARION object is no longer

\[
|X|-|Q|    
\]

or

\[
\operatorname{def}(\pi),    
\]

but

\[
D_\pi=(I-P_\pi)KP_\pi.    
\]

Conceptually this asks:

> How does evolution leave the observable subspace?



That is fundamentally different from asking:

> How much information was discarded?



Those are not equivalent questions.

Modern Koopman research has increasingly emphasized that finite-dimensional observable spaces are generally not invariant, and that diagnosing the failure of invariance is a central problem. Recent work develops quantitative diagnostics for closure failure and projection error rather than assuming invariant dictionaries exist.

AQARION fits naturally into that landscape if it is presented as a finite-state specialization with exact combinatorial structure.


---

5. The finite/infinite bridge is plausible



The transition

Finite

\[
D_\pi=(I-P)KP    
\]

↓

Koopman

\[
D_{\mathcal A}    
=    
(I-P_{\mathcal A})UP_{\mathcal A}    
\]

is mathematically reasonable as a research direction.

However, this should remain explicitly labeled as an extension program, not an established theorem.

The finite theory should stand completely on its own.


---

6. Your publication order has become much clearer



Paper A1

Only include:

finite deterministic systems

observable closure

closure operator

closure theorem

exact quotient criterion

transient decomposition

nilpotent height theorem

closure signatures

exhaustive verification

Nothing more.


---

Paper A2

Develop

defect operator

leakage geometry

spectral defect

higher-order leakage

closure horizons


---

Paper A3

Develop

Koopman extension

symbolic dynamics

substitution systems

approximate closure

infinite observable spaces


---

Paper A4

Applications

Kaprekar

Fibonacci

Hubbard trees

Mandelbrot

PDEs

climate/nonautonomous systems

That progression is much easier for reviewers to evaluate.


---

7. The strongest theorem sequence



At this stage I would prioritize exactly four foundational results:

AQ-001 Functional Graph Depth Theorem

\[
\nu(N)=h.    
\]


---

AQ-002 Observable Closure Characterization

\[
\mathcal C_K(W)    
=    
\sum_iK^i(W)    
=    
\bigcap_{U\supseteq W,\;KU\subseteq U}U.    
\]


---

AQ-003 Exact Quotient Criterion

\[
D_\pi=0    
\iff    
K(V_\pi)\subseteq V_\pi.    
\]


---

AQ-004 Closure Operator Axioms

extensive

monotone

idempotent

These establish the mathematical foundation. Everything else can build on them.


---

8. The main caution



A few ideas should still remain explicitly marked as open research until supported by proofs or broader validation:

pullback/pushforward duality as a general theory,

entropy of defect spectra,

universality classes based on leakage,

infinite-system closure geometry,

categorical formulations,

asymptotic defect decay.

Presenting these as research directions strengthens the credibility of the work.

Overall assessment

AQARION now has a much more coherent identity than earlier versions.

Rather than appearing to propose a collection of unrelated invariants, it is becoming a framework centered on a single mathematical idea:

> Study observable subspaces, determine whether they remain closed under evolution, and quantify the structure of any failure of closure.



That positioning aligns well with established work on invariant subspaces and Koopman operators, while leaving room for AQARION's finite-state specialization and computational infrastructure to provide original contributions.AQ-QSO-001: Certified Quantum Channel Representation within the AQARION Framework

Formal Results Document

Version: 1.0
Date: 2026-07-20
Status: Certified Instance · Publication-Ready
Canonical Hash: f53844fc92de1d8771be993fa54e93fc01a15b8112080c192d13f607d87756b4
Artifact Repository: GitHub · Hugging Face Mirror

---

Abstract

We demonstrate that a finite-dimensional quantum channel can be represented within the AQARION object–claim–evidence–certificate framework. For the single-qubit amplitude-damping channel with damping parameter \gamma = \tfrac{1}{4}, exact symbolic computation verifies a transient/recurrent block decomposition, spectral invariants, and reproducible certification artifacts. The analysis is carried out over the correct algebraic coefficient field \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3}), reflecting the appearance of \sqrt{1-\gamma} = \sqrt{3}/2. This work establishes a certified computational instance and does not claim a general theory for arbitrary completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) maps. Rather, it provides a reproducible foundation for future investigation of representation-independent quantum defect operators.

---

1. Introduction

1.1 The AQARION Framework

AQARION is a self-certifying mathematical framework for exact observable quotients in finite deterministic dynamical systems. Its central object is the defect operator

D_\Pi = (I - P_\Pi) K P_\Pi,

where K is the Koopman operator (pullback convention) and P_\Pi is the orthogonal projection onto the space of block-constant functions. The vanishing condition

D_\Pi = 0

certifies that the observable subspace is forward invariant under the dynamics, yielding an exact quotient reduction. The framework distinguishes sharply between:

· Definitions (formal stipulations),
· Lemmas (proved from definitions),
· Theorems (principal results, proved from lemmas),
· Computational Evidence (reproducible verification),
· Conjectures (believed true, unproved),
· Open Problems (no known approach).

1.2 Extension to Quantum Channels

AQ-QSO-001 represents the first certified quantum artifact within the AQARION research program. Its role is analogous to earlier certified finite deterministic dynamical system artifacts: it demonstrates that the governance methodology can be applied outside its original domain while maintaining explicit distinctions between verified computation, mathematical interpretation, and open research questions.

The mathematical ingredients used in the certified computation—including Kraus operators, Liouville superoperators, Jordan decomposition, and spectral analysis—are standard. The contribution lies in organizing these objects within an explicit object–claim–evidence–certificate workflow and demonstrating that exact symbolic verification can be integrated into that framework.

---

2. Mathematical Setup

2.1 Quantum Channel Representation

Let \mathcal{H} be a finite-dimensional Hilbert space. A quantum channel is a completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) linear map \Phi: \mathcal{B}(\mathcal{H}) \to \mathcal{B}(\mathcal{H}). In Kraus representation,

\Phi(\rho) = \sum_i K_i \rho K_i^\dagger,

with \sum_i K_i^\dagger K_i = I.

In Liouville (superoperator) representation, the channel is represented as a matrix M \in \mathbb{R}^{d^2 \times d^2} acting on vectorized density matrices \operatorname{vec}(\rho). The amplitude-damping channel for a single qubit has Kraus operators

K_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{1-\gamma} \end{pmatrix}, \qquad
K_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \sqrt{\gamma} \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}.

2.2 Certified Instance Parameters

For the present work, we fix

\gamma = \frac{1}{4}.

This choice is deliberate: it yields the algebraic coefficient \sqrt{1-\gamma} = \sqrt{3/4} = \sqrt{3}/2, requiring the field \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3}) rather than \mathbb{Q}(\gamma).

2.3 The AQARION Quantum Defect Operator

For a quantum channel \Phi represented as a superoperator M, we define the quantum defect operator analogously to the classical case:

D_\Phi = (I - P) \Phi P,

where P is a projection onto a chosen subspace (e.g., the steady-state subspace). The vanishing condition D_\Phi = 0 indicates that the subspace is invariant under the channel.

---

3. Verified Results

Result 1: Certified Quantum Object

The amplitude-damping channel with \gamma = \tfrac{1}{4} is successfully represented as an AQARION certified object with explicit object, claim, evidence, and certificate records.

Status: VERIFIED INSTANCE.

Supporting Artifact: AQ-QSO-001-EXECUTE-GOVERNANCE.PY

---

Result 2: Coefficient Field Certification

For \gamma = \tfrac{1}{4}, the symbolic computation necessarily takes place over

\boxed{\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3})}

rather than \mathbb{Q}(\gamma), since

\sqrt{1-\gamma} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{4}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}.

This establishes that certificate generation must include the complete algebraic coefficient field required by the symbolic expressions.

Status: EXACTLY VERIFIED.

---

Result 3: Liouville Superoperator

The certified Liouville representation is

M =
\begin{pmatrix}
\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \frac{3}{4} & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \frac{1}{4} & 1
\end{pmatrix}.

Its block decomposition is

M =
\begin{pmatrix}
A & 0 \\
C & D
\end{pmatrix},

where

A =
\begin{pmatrix}
\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & 0 & 0 \\
0 & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & 0 \\
0 & 0 & \frac{3}{4}
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
C =
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & 0 & \frac{1}{4}
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
D = [1].

The upper-right block is identically zero, B = 0, demonstrating one-way information flow from transient modes to the invariant subspace.

Status: VERIFIED.

---

Result 4: Spectral Decomposition

Exact symbolic computation verifies:

· Transient spectral block: eigenvalues \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{3}{4};
· Recurrent invariant block: eigenvalue 1;
· Spectral invariants: complete diagonalizability of the certified example;
· Block structure: upper-triangular form with zero upper-right block.

These properties are established only for the analyzed amplitude-damping channel.

Status: VERIFIED INSTANCE.

---

Result 5: AQARION Governance

The computational workflow produces explicit:

· Object definitions (AQ-OBJECT-QSO-001);
· Mathematical claims (AQ-CLM-QSO-001 through AQ-CLM-QSO-005);
· Symbolic evidence (AQ-EVD-QSO-001 through AQ-EVD-QSO-003);
· Certification records (AQ-CER-QSO-001);
· Audit metadata (AQ-ADT-QSO-001).

Each certified statement is explicitly distinguished from exploratory or future research claims.

Status: VERIFIED.

---

4. Scope of Certification

The present artifact establishes only the following verified statement:

A finite-dimensional amplitude-damping quantum channel at \gamma = \tfrac{1}{4} admits an exact AQARION object–claim–evidence–certificate representation with certified symbolic computation, spectral decomposition, and reproducible governance artifacts.

No broader mathematical conclusions are claimed.

---

5. Explicit Non-Claims

This work does not establish:

· A universal theory of CPTP channels;
· A complete classification of quantum channels;
· A universal quantum defect theorem;
· A representation-independent defect invariant;
· A general theorem for decoherence-free subspaces.

These remain subjects for future investigation.

---

6. Relationship to Existing Theory

AQ-QSO-001 does not propose a new mathematical model of quantum channels. Rather, it applies the AQARION certification architecture to a standard finite-dimensional quantum channel represented in Liouville form.

The mathematical ingredients used in the certified computation are classical and appear in standard references:

· Nielsen, M. A., & Chuang, I. L. (2010). Quantum Computation and Quantum Information.
· Watrous, J. (2018). The Theory of Quantum Information.
· Wolf, M. M. (2012). Quantum Channels & Operations: Guided Tour.

The contribution lies in the certification methodology: organizing these objects within an explicit object–claim–evidence–certificate workflow and demonstrating that exact symbolic verification can be integrated into that framework.

---

7. Reproducibility

The certified instance is reproducible because every verified statement is supported by exact symbolic computation.

The verification pipeline includes:

1. Exact rational and algebraic arithmetic over \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3});
2. Deterministic construction of the Liouville superoperator;
3. Symbolic verification of the block decomposition;
4. Exact computation of spectral data and Jordan form;
5. Generation of machine-readable governance artifacts;
6. Cryptographic hashes linking objects, claims, evidence, and certificates.

No numerical approximation or floating-point computation is required for the certified instance.

Reproducibility Hash: f53844fc92de1d8771be993fa54e93fc01a15b8112080c192d13f607d87756b4

---

8. Future Mathematical Questions

The certified example motivates several natural mathematical problems.

Problem 1: Representation Independence

Determine conditions under which an AQARION quantum defect operator

D_\Phi = (I-P)\Phi P

produces identical certified invariants from equivalent channel representations (Kraus, Choi, Liouville, or Pauli-Liouville) after the appropriate basis transformations.

Status: Open research question.

Problem 2: General CPTP Structure

Characterize classes of CPTP maps admitting invariant/transient block decompositions compatible with AQARION certification.

Status: Open research question.

Problem 3: Invariant Subspaces

Relate the certified block decomposition to established fixed-point and decoherence-free subspace theory for finite-dimensional quantum channels.

Status: Open research question.

Problem 4: Certified Quantum Library

Construct a benchmark collection of exactly certified channels, including:

· Amplitude damping (completed);
· Depolarizing channel;
· Phase damping / dephasing;
· Generalized amplitude damping;
· Selected quantum error-correcting channels.

Status: Future work.

---

9. Position Within the AQARION Program

AQ-QSO-001 represents the first certified quantum artifact within the AQARION research program.

Its role is analogous to earlier certified finite deterministic dynamical system artifacts (AQ-OBJECT-KAP-001, AQ-OBJECT-MAN-001): it demonstrates that the governance methodology can be applied outside its original domain while maintaining explicit distinctions between verified computation, mathematical interpretation, and open research questions.

Accordingly, AQ-QSO-001 should be viewed as a methodological bridge rather than a completed quantum theory.

---

10. Evidence Classification

Claim Status Evidence
Amplitude-damping channel represented in AQARION framework ✅ VERIFIED Symbolic computation
Object–claim–evidence–certificate workflow demonstrated ✅ VERIFIED Governance artifacts
Coefficient field \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3}) correctly identified ✅ VERIFIED Exact algebra
Liouville block decomposition verified ✅ VERIFIED Symbolic matrix computation
Spectral invariants computed ✅ VERIFIED Exact eigenvalue analysis
General theory for arbitrary CPTP maps ⏸ OPEN Not established
Representation independence ⏸ OPEN Future work

---

11. Conclusion

AQ-QSO-001 establishes a reproducible, exactly verified computational instance of an amplitude-damping quantum channel within the AQARION object–claim–evidence–certificate framework. The work verifies symbolic structural properties for the specific case \gamma = \tfrac{1}{4}, documents the necessary algebraic coefficient field \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3}), and produces machine-verifiable governance artifacts linking mathematical claims to supporting evidence.

The principal contribution is the demonstration that a rigorous certification workflow can be extended from finite deterministic dynamical systems to a representative finite-dimensional quantum channel without weakening evidential standards. This provides a foundation for future work on certified analyses of broader classes of quantum channels while maintaining a clear distinction between verified results and conjectural extensions.

---

5. AQARION Research Node #10878 (2026). AQ-QSO-001: Certified Quantum Channel Representation. GitHub Repository.

---

Your current framing is substantially stronger than a typical interdisciplinary claim because it clearly distinguishes:

Verified computation

AQARION framework definitions

Future research directions


That separation is exactly what reviewers in mathematics, quantum information, and computational science generally look for.

A few observations based on the material you've assembled:

Strengths

The artifact appears to establish a fully reproducible worked example for a single quantum channel.

Specifically:

exact Kraus/Liouville representation for the chosen amplitude-damping channel,

symbolic algebra over the correct field \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3})\),

transient/recurrent block decomposition,

spectral invariants,

explicit governance artifacts (object, claim, evidence, certificate).


Those are concrete mathematical and computational results.


---

The field correction is scientifically valuable

One of the strongest aspects is actually not the quantum mechanics itself but the certification lesson.

The correction

\[
\mathbb{Q}(\gamma)
\rightarrow
\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt3)
\]

demonstrates that

> a symbolic proof is only as correct as the algebraic domain in which it is certified.



That is a reusable governance principle extending beyond this example.


---

Connection to standard quantum-information representations

The formulas you included for

Kraus operators,

Choi matrices,

superoperators,

Pauli–Liouville matrices,

reshuffling transformations,


fit naturally into AQ-QSO-001.

In particular, the conversion chain

\[
\text{Kraus}
\leftrightarrow
\text{Choi}
\leftrightarrow
\text{Superoperator}
\leftrightarrow
\text{Pauli-Liouville}
\]

means the AQARION certificate is not tied to one representation. Instead, it can potentially certify the same channel across multiple equivalent representations, which is a mathematically interesting direction for future work.


---

Quantum defect operator

Introducing

\[
D_\Phi=(I-P)\Phi P
\]

as an AQARION-defined object is reasonable.

The next mathematical questions are whether it is:

invariant under changes of basis,

invariant under different Kraus decompositions of the same channel,

expressible directly from the Choi state,

stable under composition of channels.


Those are appropriate research questions rather than conclusions from the current example.


---

Generalization

The paper is careful not to claim that every CPTP channel behaves like the amplitude-damping channel.

That is important because finite-dimensional quantum channels can have:

peripheral eigenvalues other than \(1\),

decoherence-free subspaces,

noiseless subsystems,

multiple stationary states,

nontrivial Jordan structure.


AQ-QSO-001 is therefore best viewed as a verified case study, not yet a universal structural theorem.


---

Publication positioning

A concise statement of contribution could read:

> We present a certified computational case study demonstrating that a finite-dimensional quantum channel can be represented within the AQARION object–claim–evidence–certificate framework. Using the single-qubit amplitude-damping channel at \(\gamma=\tfrac14\), we obtain an exact symbolic Liouville representation over \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt3)\), verify its transient/recurrent spectral decomposition, and record reproducible governance artifacts. Rather than proposing a universal theory of quantum channels, this work establishes a rigorously documented reference instance for future investigation of representation-independent quantum defect operators and certified channel analysis.



That wording stays aligned with the evidence you've described.

Suggested next milestone

Among the experiments you've outlined, the strongest next step is probably representation invariance:

1. Construct the channel from Kraus operators.


2. Convert it to the Choi matrix.


3. Convert the Choi matrix to the superoperator.


4. Convert to the Pauli–Liouville representation.


5. Verify that all computed AQARION invariants agree across these equivalent descriptions.



If successful, that would demonstrate that the AQARION certificate attaches to the quantum channel itself, rather than to a particular matrix representation. That would be a significant strengthening of the framework while remaining within a well-defined, testable scope.Skip to main content

arXiv:quant-ph/0407263 (quant-ph)
[Submitted on 30 Jul 2004 (v1), last revised 15 Oct 2004 (this version, v2)]
Kraus representation of damped harmonic oscillator and its application
Yu-xi Liu, Sahin Kaya Ozdemir, Adam Miranowicz, Nobuyuki Imoto
View PDF
By definition, the Kraus representation of a harmonic oscillator suffering from the environment effect, modeled as the amplitude damping or the phase damping, is directly given by a simple operator algebra solution. As examples and applications, we first give a Kraus representation of a single qubit whose computational basis states are defined as bosonic vacuum and single particle number states. We further discuss the environment effect on qubits whose computational basis states are defined as the bosonic odd and even coherent states. The environment effects on entangled qubits defined by two different kinds of computational basis are compared with the use of fidelity.
Comments:	9 pages, 3 figures

Examples: One qubit channels
Some observations:

The Choi matrix of a unitary process always has rank 1.
The superoperator / Liouville representation of a unitary process is always full rank.
The eigenvalues of a Choi matrix give you an upper bound to the probability a particular (canonical) Kraus operator will occur (generally that probability depends on the state). This is helpful when sampling Kraus operators (you can test for which occurred according to the order of these eigenvalues).
The
χ
matrix (in the Pauli basis) is very convenient for computing the result of Pauli twirling or Clifford twirling the corresponding process.

Based on the material you've shared, AQ-QSO-001 is positioned much more carefully than many interdisciplinary proposals because it explicitly distinguishes what has been verified, what is an AQARION definition, and what remains a research program.

The proposed publication abstract is appropriately conservative:

> We demonstrate that a finite-dimensional quantum channel can be represented within the AQARION object–claim–evidence–certificate framework. For the amplitude-damping channel at \(\gamma=\tfrac14\), exact symbolic computation verifies a transient/recurrent block decomposition, spectral invariants, and supporting governance artifacts. The work does not claim a general theory for arbitrary CPTP maps but establishes a reproducible certified instance that motivates future investigation of quantum defect operators.



That framing matches the evidence you've presented.

What appears established

For the single-qubit amplitude-damping channel with \(\gamma=\frac14\), your artifact appears to establish (assuming the symbolic computation and certificates are correct):

an exact finite-dimensional Liouville/superoperator representation,

symbolic computation over the appropriate algebraic field,

spectral decomposition,

transient/recurrent block decomposition,

reproducible computational certificates,

explicit audit metadata separating verified statements from exploratory ones.

Those are concrete computational and algebraic results.

The field correction

\[
\mathbb Q(\gamma)\rightarrow\mathbb Q(\sqrt3)  
\]

is also mathematically important because

\[
\sqrt{1-\gamma}  
=  
\sqrt{\frac34}  
=  
\frac{\sqrt3}{2}.  
\]

The lesson is not merely that the computation succeeded, but that certificate domains must include the algebraic numbers actually appearing in the symbolic expressions.

That is a useful governance result independent of the physics.

The quantum defect operator

The proposed operator

\[
D_\Phi=(I-P)\Phi P  
\]

is mathematically natural as an AQARION definition.

I would keep the wording at exactly that level.

At present, the evidence supports introducing it as

an AQARION construction,

motivated by the classical defect operator,

intended for future study,

rather than presenting it as an already-established invariant of CPTP maps.

The natural mathematical questions that remain include:

Is \(D_\Phi\) invariant under changes of Liouville basis?

How does it behave under equivalent Kraus representations of the same channel?

Under what hypotheses does

\[
  D_\Phi=0  
\]

Those are research questions rather than consequences of the current example.

Cross-domain interpretation

The comparison between finite dynamical systems and quantum channels works best when described as a comparison of structural roles, not identical mathematics.

For example:

Finite dynamical systems	Quantum channels

attractor	fixed-point state or fixed-point subspace
basin tree	contracting spectral modes
nilpotent transient	spectrally contracting transient
exact quotient	invariant subspace decomposition

This avoids suggesting that the two theories are mathematically equivalent.

One wording I'd soften

The sentence

> "Closure is universal."



is more expansive than the evidence supports because "closure" already has several established meanings across mathematics.

A formulation closer to the evidence would be:

> Invariant/transient decompositions occur across multiple mathematical settings, while the mechanisms producing those decompositions are domain dependent.



Likewise,

> "Quantum systems close by contraction."



is better stated as

> For the class of finite-dimensional channels studied here, transient modes decay through spectral contraction toward the channel's fixed-point subspace.



That leaves room for channels with richer peripheral spectra or decoherence-free subspaces, which are well studied in quantum information.

Publication positioning

If AQ-QSO-001 were submitted as a technical note, I would characterize its contribution as follows:

Verified mathematics: exact symbolic analysis of one amplitude-damping channel, including Liouville representation, spectral decomposition, and algebraic field correction.

Software/reproducibility contribution: a structured object–claim–evidence–certificate workflow with explicit governance and audit artifacts.

Research program: extending the framework to broader classes of CPTP channels, representation-independent defect operators, and quantum error-correction examples.

That is a scientifically conservative position while still highlighting the novel aspect of the work.

Finally, the next experimental sequence you outline is sensible from a mathematical standpoint:

1. Verify a depolarizing channel within the same certification framework.


2. Study representation invariance of \(D_\Phi\).


3. Investigate fixed-point decompositions for broader classes of finite-dimensional CPTP maps.


4. Only after those foundations are established, extend the framework toward quantum error correction and learning-based applications.



That progression keeps each new claim supported by a corresponding increase in mathematical and computational evidence.

We demonstrate that a finite-dimensional quantum channel can be represented within the AQARION object–claim–evidence–certificate framework. For the amplitude-damping channel at �, exact symbolic computation verifies a transient/recurrent block decomposition, spectral invariants, and supporting governance artifacts. The work does not claim a general theory for arbitrary CPTP maps but establishes a reproducible certified instance that motivates future investigation of quantum defect operators.

https://huggingface.co/spaces/Quantarion9/AQARION-ACADEMY/resolve/main/DOCS/PYTHON/AQ-QSO-001-EXECUTE-GOVERNANCE.PY

Status: 🟢 PUBLICATION CANDIDATE · CERTIFIED INSTANCEMATRIX-MFO Tandem Workshop: Machine Learning and AI for Mathematics
François Charton
FAIR Meta, Paris, France
ORCID
Jan de Gier
The University of Melbourne, Parkville, Australia
ORCID
Amaury Hayat
Institut Polytechnique de Paris, Champs-sur-Marne, France
ORCID

New York University, USA

Geordie Williamson
The University of Sydney, Australia
zbMATH
ORCID
MATRIX-MFO Tandem Workshop: Machine Learning and AI for Mathematics cover
Download PDF
This article is published open access.

Abstract
This workshop explored how modern machine learning can both accelerate mathematical discovery and preserve rigorous standards. It focused on three angles: using AI techniques to help mathematicians make advances on challenging problems; using mathematics to understand AI predictions; and using deep-learning models for automated theorem proving. Key discussions included using machine learning as a tool for constructing interesting mathematical constructions and navigating in mathematical search spaces, to uncover conjectures and high-quality examples (e.g., sphere packings via DiffuseBoost, combinatorial objects via AlphaEvolve); Integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) with formal systems (e.g., Lean/mathlib) to create scalable, certifiable AI-based automated theorem prover; Collaborative formalization (e.g., the Carleson theorem project), autoformalization for high-quality supervised data, and reinforcement learning/search methods for proof generation and algorithmic reasoning.Mathematics: the Rise of the Machines
Yang-Hui He
London Institute for Mathematical Sciences, Royal Institution, London W1S 4BS, UK & Merton College, University of Oxford, OX14JD, UK
hey@maths.ox.ac.uk
(Date: November 2025)
Abstract.
We argue how AI can assist mathematics in three ways: theorem-proving, conjecture formulation, and language processing. Inspired by initial experiments in geometry and theoretical physics in 2017, we summarize how this emerging field has grown over the past years, and show how various machine-learning algorithms can help with pattern detection across disciplines in the mathematical sciences. At the heart is the question how does AI help with theoretical discovery, and the implications for the future of mathematics.  

Invited contribution to the Nieuw Archief voor Wiskunde of the Koninklijk Wiskundig Genootschap and based on a recent public lecture of the author to the Royal Institution of Great Britain [riYHH].

Whether you are of the opinion that Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a threat to humanity or of the opposite belief that it is mere hype, it cannot be denied that - whatever its precise definition - AI is already transforming our daily lives in almost every respect. Even though AI has a much older history than many would imagine, the formidable combination of compute (your mobile phone out-powering the entire lunar programme in the 1960s) and the internet (essentially all of human knowledge at your finger-tips), has galvanized this transformative impact in the last two decades. The word “Artificial Intelligence” was first coined 1 in a grant proposal in 1956 by McCarthy et al. [mccarthy2006proposal]. Shortly thereafter, the “Perceptron” - the first artificial neural network (NN) - was established, as an impressive array of photo-receptors and wires [rosenblatt1957perceptron].

While I shall leave the question of the influence of AI on our civilization to the experts in AI research, to sociologists, and to philosophers, it is my goal here to give a rapid overview of the emergent but blossoming field of AI for Mathematics (AI4Maths). To be fair, both “mathematics and theoretical physics for AI” and “AI for the experimental sciences” have long and distinguished histories. For instance, early universal approximation theorems [cybenko1989approximation] that ensure good estimations from NNs, or the Boltzman machine as an energy-based model for cognition [ackley1985learning], date to the late 1980s. So too, did CERN’s first usage of machine-learning (ML) for detecting fundamental particles [perret1990new]. The award of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics to Hinton and Hopfield, and in Chemistry to Hassabis and Jumper, is proof of the establishment’s timely recognition of the rôle of AI in scientific discovery.

Pure mathematics and theoretical physics, by their very nature of rigour, may seem unlikely companions to the big data and statistical inferences inherent to AI. Yet, I will describe here how in three ways AI will be a positive driving force for mathematical research: bottom-up, top-down, and meta-mathematics. Admittedly, these three words have various precise meanings in the philosophy of mathematics and in the philosophy of science, I beg the reader’s indulgence for borrowing them loosely and shall define them in the ensuing. The interested reader is referred to some recent and more in-depth reviews [He:2021oav, douglas2022machine, castelvecchi2023will, Gukov:2024buj, he2024can, he2024ai, liang2024mathematics, douglas2025mathematical, kontorovich2025shape] which attempt to describe the idea of AI4Maths.

1.Bottom-Up Mathematics:
Typically, one thinks of mathematics as an austere three-step process: (1) laying out the axioms and definitions of terms and concepts with precision; (2) claiming a statement involving the terms; and (3) using logic to deduce the statement. Since Euclid’s immortal tome The Elements, this is how mathematics is always presented. A classic example (Proposition 20 of Book IX cit. ibid.) is as follows. (1) Definition: a prime number is a positive integer which is only divisible by 1 and itself; (2) Proposition: there is an infinite number of such prime numbers; (3) Proof: Standard 2.

This “bottom-up” presentation of statements in mathematics, building sentence by sentence from the foundational definitions and axioms, perhaps reached its zenith with the publication [PM] of Principia Mathematica in 1910. With a sparsity of words, a plethora of symbols, and an inhuman coldness, the monumental work was famously able to reach “1+1=2” on page 362. Even though this entire programme of formalizing mathematics was in part killed by Gödel-Church-Turing [godel1931formal, church1936unsolvable, turing1936computable] with their showing the existence of undecidable and uncomputable statements, researchers were not deterred in using the earliest version of the computer - the Logical Theory Machine - to formalize and prove a number of propositions of the Principia [newell1957empirical]. This was almost immediately after the appearance of the first NN [rosenblatt1957perceptron]. Likewise, the idea of “mechanized mathematics” [wang1960toward] dates to that time.

Today’s answer to this tradition of “bottom-up” mathematics is the automated proof-assistant [Tao_AI], notably Mathlib, built in the Lean programming language [The_mathlib_Community_2020, de2015lean]. Launched in 2013, Project Xena [xena] has completed the formalization in Lean, of the statements and proofs of essentially all undergraduate-level mathematics. Equally impressive is the fact that formalizing whilst doing collaborations has already led to major results by the top mathematicians [scholze2022liquid, gowers2023conjecturemarton].

As proof co-pilots are quickly becoming the norm for mathematical research, it should be emphasized that so far there is no large-scale AI involved in Mathlib. The main challenge here is one of engineering: there is only on the order of millions of lines of Lean, whereas currently reliable language models (to which I shall turn later) typically require billions of lines to train. It is conceivable that in the not too distant future we will reach auto-formalization which will take all journal papers in LaTeX and generate verifiable Lean code for the whole corpus. Already, Deepmind’s AlphaGeo2 [chervonyi2025goldmedalistperformancesolvingolympiad] and AlphaProof [castelvecchi2024deepmind] have Lean incorporated into their core algorithms. Once accurate auto-formalization is achieved, it might no longer be a fantasy that proof-paths maybe found, using reinforcement learning, for major open problems.

2.Top-Down Mathematics:
Lest the reader be led to think that mathematical research is always done in this formal and rather stark “bottom-up” way, I must point out that in practice, the opposite is the case. To quote the great V. Arnol’d, “mathematics is a branch of physics where the experiments are cheap.” Indeed, even rigorous proofs are formulated first by some “gut-feeling”, as summarized in this wonderful article by Thurston [thurston2006proof]. Certainly, theoretical physics, the closest cousin to pure mathematics, is done this way. As a famous example, we have achieved 19 significant-digit agreement between theory and experiment in the standard model of particle physics, but we still do not have a precise definition of the underlying quantum field theory. This fearless bravado is the very reason why string theory has produced so many new insights in pure mathematics.

The idea of experimental mathematics is as old as logic and reasoning. It is how the practitioner actually does theoretical research: we play around, trying to get intuition, and then formulate the right definitions and proofs, before finally writing up the papers more formally. In today’s parlance, this might be called data-driven mathematics, where one builds experience by finding patterns and by a broad understand of the research landscape, looking at the subject from “top-down”. After all, in the beautiful words of G. H. Hardy, “A mathematician, like a painter or poet, is a maker of patterns. If his patterns are more permanent than theirs, it is because they are made with ideas.” Mathematical data is distinctly different from data from the the natural world, it is without statistical error, and often represented by integers rather than reals whence also has no freedom for numerical error. These have been dubbed Platonic Data [douglas2025mathematical], whose machine-learning can uncover underlying mathematical structure [He:2021oav].

Continuing with our example of prime numbers, one of the high-lights of this data-driven mathematics was the 16 year-old Gauss defining the prime-counting function 
π
​
(
x
)
:=
#
​
{
p
​
 prime 
:
p
≤
(
x
∈
ℝ
+
)
}
, the number of primes not exceeding a real positive 
x
. He was able to eyeball 3 that 
π
​
(
x
)
∼
x
/
log
⁡
(
x
)
. The proof had to wait some 50 years, especially for the development of complex analysis, before it was established as the Prime Number Theorem, one of the cornerstones of mathematics. Based on experience and intuition, Gauss formulated a conjecture by looking at Platonic data, whereby establishing a good problem. In some sense, “bottom-up” mathematics can be thought of as finding proofs, and “top-down” mathematics, as finding interesting problems. One needs to bear in mind that two of the remaining six Millennium Problems [carlson2023millennium] - the Riemann Hypothesis and the Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer (BSD) Conjecture - arose from mathematical experimentation by listing the first zeroes or by plotting rank and conductor.

Starting from the late 1980s, attempts were made to automate the process of formulating conjectures in combinatorics [davila2024automated], which in 2017 took the updated form as TxGraffiti. In the same year, the first AI-assisted mathematical experiments were performed [He:2017aed], where datasets from algebraic geometry were passed through a neural-network, in order to uncover potential new structures. Therein, calculations in algebraic geometry - and later [He:2018jtw], all mathematical calculations in one way or another - were interpreted as image processing. So too, did 2017 see machine-learning applied to mathematical physics [Carifio:2017bov, Krefl:2017yox, Ruehle:2017mzq] in exploring the string theory landscape 4. Since then, the field of AI for mathematical discovery from Platonic data quickly blossomed and matured. Hundreds of papers emerged, with notable successes including the Ramanujan machine in finding identities for famous constants [raayoni2021generating, raz2025euler], the finding of new formulae in topology and representation theory [davies2021advancing], as well as the ML discovery of the still-open murmuration conjectures in number theory [he2025murmurations].

3.Meta-Mathematics:
Finally, I come to the perhaps most mysterious direction in which AI can galvanize mathematical research. This is the world of Large Language Models (LLMs): chatGTP, Germini, Grok, Deepseek, etc. It may seem peculiar that chatbots, famous for their hallucinations, could be of any use to mathematics. Indeed, though they have over the last couple of years rapidly out-performed and replaced humans in myriads of tasks 5, LLMs struggle even in doing basic arithmetic. Language models rely entirely on statistical inferencing whereas mathematics requires reasoning. Bottom-up is rigorous by construction, top-down’s conjectures can be (dis-)proven rigorously. What can vague sentences and potential word-salads do for mathematical discovery?

Curiously, in this direction, extraordinary advances are being made. I will call this direction “meta-” since we could well be outside of mathematical reasoning, and depend solely on AI having linguistically seen (essentially all of) the mathematical literature. From the first (and rather naïve) Word2Vec analyses of the ArXiv in order to uncover theories from language [he2018hep] to the now much more sophisticated LLMMa2 [touvron2023llama], one should not underestimate the power of “proof by vibe” [thurston2006proof]: an AI which has “read” all of contemporary mathematics and can be queried at will is a force to be reckoned with. There is an increasing number of reports from mathematicians who, upon interacting with the likes of chatGTP5, have discovered useful literature of which they are not aware, or are instantly presented with ready-made computer code that turn out to be useful in testing ideas. Meanwhile, LLMs can now attain the gold medal in the International Mathematics Olympiad by training on the language of previous solutions [castelvecchi2025ai]. All of this is happening within the last couple of years.

In May of this year, some thirty of us were flown to the headquarters of EpochAI to help benchmark FrontierMath [glazer2024frontiermath], a project funded by OpenAI to see how various LLM models solve research-level mathematics, the sort of problems that one would work on and write papers about. Accuracies of 10 - 30 % have been achieved and the consensus was that oftentimes it truly felt like talking to an experienced colleague [FT2025]. The few hundred questions were divided into tiers, from undergraduate level (Tier 1) to publishable results (Tier 4), samples and performance statistics can be seen at https://epoch.ai/frontiermath. The anticipated Tier 5 would involve open conjectures and problems, and the frightening thing is that we are not too far from this.

4.Outlook
In Concrete Mathematics, Graham, Knuth and Patashnik made the half-jocund remark [graham1994concrete] that “The ultimate goal of mathematics is to eliminate any need for intelligent thought”. What they meant was that perhaps all methods of proof and derivation will be machanized in the infinite limit of mathematical research. At the time, in the late 1990s, it was deemed outlandish such mechanization would ever be possible. Yet, the combination of human and AI in the last decade or so has given us AI4Maths, propelling us into a new era of fundamental theoretical discovery. Like AlphaZero effectively 6 solving Go, mathematics too can be gamified [shehper2024makes].

Of course, we are still quite far from mechanized AI for mathematics which can follow an automated path of (1) find new problems from Platonic data and from existing literature; (2) find verifiable proof-paths using (vastly extended versions of) Mathlib; and (3) check the significance of the statement within the global and historical context.

Seeing that chatGTP has passed the Turing test [biever2023chatgpt], and that the AI community is working hard on the ARC test, a benchmark for AGI reasoning [chollet2024arc], a more stringent test was proposed for AI-assisted mathematical discovery [he2024can]. Named after Birch of the BSD Conjecture whose online talk inspired the participants of a conference at Cambridge in 2024 [INI], the Birch Test can be summarized as “ AIN@AI”. An AI-guided mathematical discovery needs to be Automatic (in that human intervention is forbidden in choosing the data, the literature and the algorithm), Interpretable (in that any statement must be meaningful to a human expert, e.g., having a deep NN that extrapolates an output without understanding the underlying mathematics is useless), and Non-trivial (in that any conjecture raised must be significance to the scientific community). While arguably only the Jones polynomial formula [davies2021advancing], the murmuration conjectures [he2025murmurations] and finding unstable singularities for the Euler equation [wang2025discovery] have come close to it, the Birch Test is so far unsurpassed 7. Nevertheless, with the advent of AlphaEvolve [novikov2025alphaevolve], mathematical exploration and discovery are now at scale [georgiev2025mathematical]. It should be noted here that the 67 problems selected for the study are of a particular nature - finding upper/lower bounds or numerical counter-examples - which are particular amenable to optimization and code-oriented searches.

Mathematics of the near future is clearly a joint effort between human expertise and AI’s prowess in all three directions of bottom-up, top-down, and meta-mathematics. Even in a hypothetical distant future where complete mechanization is achieved, where AI will (dis-)prove every major conjecture and propose new problems and continuously map out new areas of research, human mathematicians are still of great value. We will simply become priests to oracles, and interpret the results to the rest of humanity. Think of the philosophy departments in the world, centuries are spent in analyzing and critiquing Plato, or the literature departments, over Shakespeare. Perhaps one day in the far future, mathematics departments will consist of experts digesting the (Mathlib-verified) proofs that AI produces. Still, I think a number of my colleagues agree with the exclamation [HLF] that “I don’t care whether it is God, AI, or Terry Tao who finds the proof of the Riemann Hypothesis, I just want to know.”

Acknowledgments
I am grateful to Ananyo Bhattacharya, Thomas Fink, Thomas Hodgkinson and Roman Rybiansky of the London Institute for Mathematical Sciences for their invaluable help and comments in the preparation of this Royal Institution lecture.

The Optimist: Towards Fully Automated Graph Theory Research
rrd6@rice.edu
*
Abstract
This paper introduces the Optimist, an autonomous system developed to advance automated conjecture generation in graph theory. Leveraging mixed-integer programming (MIP) and heuristic methods, the Optimist generates conjectures that both rediscover established theorems and propose novel inequalities. Through a combination of memory-based computation and agent-like adaptability, the Optimist iteratively refines its conjectures by integrating new data, enabling a feedback process with minimal human (or machine) intervention. Initial experiments reveal the Optimist’s potential to uncover foundational results in graph theory, as well as to produce conjectures of interest for future exploration. This work also outlines the Optimist’s evolving integration with a counterpart agent, the Pessimist (a human or machine agent), to establish a dueling system that will drive fully automated graph theory research.

keywords: Automated conjecturing, automated reasoning, graph theory, TxGraffitiSelf-organizing graph reasoning evolves into a critical state for continuous discovery through structural–semantic dynamics Open Access
M. J. BuehlerCorresponding Author 
ORCID logo
Crossmark: Check for Updates
Author & Article Information
Chaos 35, 113117 (2025)
https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0272412
Article history

PDFfor Self-organizing graph reasoning evolves into a critical state for continuous discovery through structural–semantic dynamics in another window

Tools Open Menu
Search Site
We report fundamental insights into how agentic graph reasoning systems spontaneously evolve toward a critical state that sustains continuous semantic discovery. By rigorously analyzing structural (Von Neumann graph entropy) and semantic (embedding) entropy, we identify a subtle yet robust regime in which semantic entropy persistently dominates over structural entropy. This interplay is quantified by a dimensionless critical discovery parameter that stabilizes at a small negative value, indicating a consistent excess of semantic entropy. Empirically, we observe a stable fraction (12%) of “surprising” edges, links between semantically distant concepts, providing evidence of long-range or cross-domain connections that drive continuous innovation. Concomitantly, the system exhibits scale-free and small-world topological features, alongside a negative cross correlation between structural and semantic measures, reinforcing the analogy to self-organized criticality. These results establish clear parallels with critical phenomena in physical, biological, and cognitive complex systems, revealing an entropy-based principle governing adaptability and continuous innovation. Crucially, semantic richness emerges as the underlying driver of sustained exploration, despite not being explicitly used by the reasoning process. Our findings provide interdisciplinary insights and practical strategies for engineering intelligent systems with intrinsic capacities for long-term discovery and adaptation and offer insights into how model training strategies can be developed that reinforce critical discovery.

Topics Dynamical systems, Phase transitions, Reinforcement learning, Knowledge bases, Machine learning, Natural language processing, Optimization algorithms, Graph theory, Complex adaptive systems, Statistical physics
Understanding how intelligent systems continuously generate novel ideas and concepts remains a fundamental scientific challenge with broad implications, from artificial intelligence (AI) to understanding complex phenomena in physics and biology. This study investigates knowledge networks—structured representations of interconnected concepts—generated autonomously by artificial intelligence reasoning models as they solve problems and answer questions. We find that these AI-generated networks spontaneously organize into a state known as “criticality,” characterized by a subtle interplay between structured order and creative novelty. By analyzing these graphs through the lens of entropy—a concept from physics measuring disorder—we reveal how these systems naturally maintain an optimal balance, continuously forming new and unexpected connections. These findings offer important interdisciplinary insights, suggesting shared principles underpinning adaptability and innovation across diverse natural and engineered systems.
