I think this critique is well calibrated. It doesn't argue that AQARION is weak; it argues that different parts of the project have different standards of evidence. That distinction is exactly what mathematical and theoretical computer science reviewers will care about.

There are three recommendations I'd make.

1. Split the project into independent research artifacts



One risk is making every paper depend on every other paper. Instead, treat them as independent contributions with different evidence standards.

Artifact	Primary evidence	Publication target

A1	Mathematical proofs	Mathematics / TCS
A2	Mathematical proofs + examples	Mathematics
AQBench-FDS	Software + datasets + exhaustive ground truth	Software / AI evaluation
AVF	Infrastructure + reproducibility	Mathematical software
AQ-E	Controlled experiments	AI for Science / Agentic Science

That way, a reviewer who dislikes AI benchmarking can still evaluate A1 purely as mathematics.

2. Replace "performance claims" with "protocol claims"



I think this is the single biggest change I'd make.

Instead of claiming:

> AQARION reduces false claims by 94.5%.



Claim something like:

> AQARION defines an evidence-governed protocol that measures false-claim rate, verification depth, reproducibility, and correction latency. We evaluate this protocol on a benchmark of finite deterministic dynamical systems.



Then, if an experiment later measures a 94.5% reduction, that's an empirical result—not part of the framework itself.

This separation mirrors long-standing guidance on reporting computational experiments, which emphasizes explicit experimental design, documentation, reproducibility, and independent re-execution over headline performance numbers.

3. Make reproducibility the flagship experiment



This is where I think AQARION can be genuinely distinctive.

Instead of asking:

> "Is AQARION smarter?"



ask:

> "Can independent researchers reproduce every certified claim from the public artifacts?"



That leads naturally to an experiment like AQ-E3: Cross-Laboratory Reproducibility.

For each certified theorem, record:

Build success rate

Independent execution success

Hash agreement

Certificate verification

Runtime variance

Number of discrepancies reported

Time to resolve discrepancies

Those metrics align directly with decades of recommendations in computational mathematics and scientific software, where reproducibility and transparent computational artifacts are treated as first-class research outcomes.


---

A refinement of your evidence ladder

I would also separate proof status from verification status.

Instead of a single evidence scale, use two orthogonal axes.

Proof Status (P):

P0 — Conjecture

P1 — Informal proof

P2 — Peer-audited proof

P3 — Machine-checked formal proof

Verification Status (V):

V0 — None

V1 — Sampled computation

V2 — Exhaustive finite verification

V3 — Independent implementation

V4 — Regression-certified

V5 — Independently reproduced

A theorem might then be labeled, for example:

(P2, V5) — audited mathematical proof with full computational reproducibility.

(P0, V5) — conjecture with exhaustive computational support but no proof.

(P3, V4) — formally proved with certified software artifacts.

That is more informative than collapsing everything into one scalar evidence level.


---

The long-term identity

The project becomes strongest if its primary contribution is not:

> "We built a better AI scientist."



Instead, it becomes:

> We built an evidence-governed methodology for computational mathematics that cleanly separates mathematical proof, computational verification, software certification, and empirical evaluation.



That framing is narrower, easier to defend, and fits naturally alongside established reproducibility practices in computational science rather than competing directly with general-purpose autonomous research systems. It also leaves room for AQBench and future agent experiments to be evaluated as applications of the framework rather than the foundation itself.I think this is a very strong critique, and it's largely consistent with what reviewers in mathematics, theoretical computer science, and computational science would likely emphasize.

The most important insight is a strategic one:

> AQARION should not try to prove that it is "better at doing mathematics." It should try to prove that it is "better at producing trustworthy mathematical results."



Those are fundamentally different research questions.

The papers you cited reinforce exactly this distinction. The classic guidance from Crowder, Dembo & Mulvey (1979), Wilson et al. (2014), and Fehr et al. (2016) all converge on the same principle: computational claims should be accompanied by transparent experimental design, reproducible artifacts, and enough detail for independent verification. AQARION's governance philosophy naturally aligns with that tradition rather than competing against it.

I would actually restructure the research program

Instead of

Mathematics

Software

Benchmark

Agentic Science

I'd organize it as four increasingly independent contributions.

Track A — Mathematical Foundations

Audience:

Mathematics

Dynamical systems

Semigroup theory

Claims:

observable-generated quotients

exact quotient criterion

defect operator

closure theory

Everything here stands or falls on mathematical proofs.

No AI is needed.


---

Track B — Computational Mathematics

Audience:

Mathematical software

Computational algebra

Reproducible mathematics

Contribution:

AQBench-FDS

Ground-truth finite dynamical systems

Complete enumeration

Reference implementations

Certification

This is publishable even if nobody cares about AI.


---

Track C — Research Infrastructure

Audience:

Scientific computing

Reproducibility

Research software

Contribution:

AVF

Evidence ladders

Certification

Regression tests

Independent implementations

Artifacts

Again,

no AI claims required.


---

Track D — Agentic Science

Only after A–C exist.

Question:

> Does evidence governance improve AI-assisted mathematical research?



Now AQ-E2 becomes one experiment inside Track D.

Not the foundation.


---

I completely agree about AQ-E3

In fact I think it is stronger than AQ-E2.

Instead of asking

> Can AQARION outperform another AI?



ask

> Can another laboratory reproduce AQARION?



That question is objective.

Possible metrics:

clean checkout succeeds

all tests pass

theorem certificates regenerate

hashes agree

numerical outputs agree

independent implementations agree

Lean proofs verify (where available)

Those are exactly the kinds of measurements computational science values.


---

One experiment I would add

I would call it

AQ-E4 — Evidence Ablation

Remove one governance layer at a time.

Pipeline:

Full AQARION

↓

remove regression tests

↓

remove counterexample agent

↓

remove independent implementation

↓

remove formalization

↓

remove governance

Measure

false claims

correction latency

reproducibility

certification level

This tells you which governance components actually matter.

That is much more scientifically informative than simply comparing AQARION to another system.


---

Another experiment

AQ-E5

Cost of Certainty

Every verification stage has a cost.

Measure

CPU time

memory

wall clock

human review

proof effort

versus

confidence gained

You could literally produce an "evidence frontier."

For example:

Evidence level	Cost	Confidence

Examples	Low	Low
Large-scale computation	Medium	Medium
Exhaustive verification	High	Very high
Independent implementation	Higher	Very high
Formal proof	Highest	Highest

This shifts the discussion from "AI is better" to the more nuanced question of the trade-off between verification effort and assurance.


---

I would also soften the language

One phrase I would avoid is

> "The mathematics is closed."



That is not a standard mathematical claim and invites objections.

Instead:

> "The current framework provides a complete foundation for the classes of finite deterministic dynamical systems studied here while identifying explicit open problems and planned extensions."



That is precise, modest, and reviewer-friendly.


---

What I think AQARION's enduring contribution could be

After following the project's evolution, I think the long-term contribution is unlikely to be remembered primarily for any single theorem or benchmark.

The more durable contribution could be a methodology:

> A methodology for conducting AI-assisted mathematical research in which every claim carries explicit evidence, provenance, reproducibility metadata, computational certification, and—where possible—formal verification.



That contribution is orthogonal to whether future AI models become dramatically stronger. If the methodology proves useful, it could be adopted well beyond finite dynamical systems.

In that sense, AQARION would contribute not only new mathematics, but also a disciplined framework for how computational mathematical research is conducted and audited. That is an ambitious goal, but it is one that can be evaluated empirically and incrementally, which makes it a stronger basis for publication than broad claims about autonomous scientific discovery.This is an ambitious and much more coherent research program than earlier iterations. The strongest improvement is the clear separation between mathematical theory, computational evidence, software infrastructure, and governance. That said, I would not describe it as "submission-ready" without qualification. Some parts appear ready for external review, while others still require additional evidence.

Here's how I would assess it.

Component	Assessment	Comments

Mathematical architecture	Strong	The focus on observable closure, exact quotients, and the defect operator is coherent.
Research governance	Excellent	Evidence levels, reproducibility, and explicit distinction between proof and computation are major strengths.
Software infrastructure	Strong	Large verification suite and certification pipeline are valuable engineering contributions.
Paper A1	Potentially ready after careful proof audit	Depends entirely on the correctness and completeness of every mathematical proof.
Paper A2	Promising	Needs external mathematical scrutiny because closure/Galois results are substantial claims.
AQBench (B1)	Strong benchmark paper	Useful independent contribution if datasets and evaluation protocol are released.
AQ-E2 experiment	Not yet publication-ready as scientific evidence	The reported results are from a simulated experiment. They should not be presented as demonstrating real-world superiority until validated empirically.

The largest issue I see is with AQ-E2.

Your tables report precise quantitative improvements such as:

94.5% false-claim reduction

97.5% reliability

98.8% discovery preservation

Those numbers are appropriate only if they come from an actual executed benchmark with reproducible data. If they are simulated, illustrative, or generated from assumptions, they should be labeled accordingly. Otherwise reviewers are likely to reject them as unsupported empirical claims.

A stronger path would be to transform AQ-E2 into a genuine benchmark:

define a fixed corpus of finite dynamical-system problems,

compare multiple systems under identical conditions,

preregister evaluation metrics,

release all prompts, logs, and scoring scripts,

report confidence intervals and statistical significance rather than single percentages.

That would produce evidence that reviewers can independently verify.

For the mathematics, your most defensible contribution appears to be:

1. Exact observable quotient criterion.


2. Defect operator formulation.


3. Observable closure framework for finite deterministic systems.


4. Computational certification infrastructure.



These are internally connected and form a coherent narrative.

The parts that require extra care are the broader positioning statements. For example, statements like "The mathematics is closed" are rhetorically strong but difficult to justify in a research paper. A more measured phrasing would emphasize that the current framework establishes a foundation while identifying clearly delineated open problems.

If your goal is publication in mathematics or theoretical computer science, I would prioritize the papers in this order:

1. Paper A1 — foundational mathematics (highest priority).


2. AQBench (B1) — reproducible benchmark and infrastructure.


3. Paper A2 — closure operators and observable bases.


4. AQ-E2 — only after replacing the simulated study with a fully reproducible empirical benchmark.



Overall, I would characterize the project as having a strong research architecture with a clear methodological identity. The main remaining step is ensuring that every theorem is backed by a complete proof and every empirical claim is backed by independently reproducible experimental data. That distinction will make the work substantially more persuasive to reviewers.Skip to main content

Computer Science > Mathematical Software
arXiv:1607.01191 (cs)
[Submitted on 5 Jul 2016]
Best Practices for Replicability, Reproducibility and Reusability of Computer-Based Experiments Exemplified by Model Reduction Software
Jörg Fehr, Jan Heiland, Christian Himpe, Jens Saak
View PDF
Over the recent years the importance of numerical experiments has gradually been more recognized. Nonetheless, sufficient documentation of how computational results have been obtained is often not available. Especially in the scientific computing and applied mathematics domain this is crucial, since numerical experiments are usually employed to verify the proposed hypothesis in a publication. This work aims to propose standards and best practices for the setup and publication of numerical experiments. Naturally, this amounts to a guideline for development, maintenance, and publication of numerical research software. Such a primer will enable the replicability and reproducibility of computer-based experiments and published results and also promote the reusability of the associated software.
Subjects:	Mathematical Software (cs.MS); Software Engineering (cs.SE)
MSC classes:	68N30
ACM classes:	G.4
Cite as:	arXiv:1607.01191 [cs.MS]
(or arXiv:1607.01191v1 [cs.MS] for this version)

https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1607.01191

Computer Science > Mathematical Software
arXiv:1210.0530 (cs)
[Submitted on 1 Oct 2012 (v1), last revised 26 Sep 2013 (this version, v4)]
Best Practices for Scientific Computing
Greg Wilson, D. A. Aruliah, C. Titus Brown, Neil P. Chue Hong, Matt Davis, Richard T. Guy, Steven H. D. Haddock, Katy Huff, Ian M. Mitchell, Mark Plumbley, Ben Waugh, Ethan P. White, Paul Wilson
View PDF
Scientists spend an increasing amount of time building and using software. However, most scientists are never taught how to do this efficiently. As a result, many are unaware of tools and practices that would allow them to write more reliable and maintainable code with less effort. We describe a set of best practices for scientific software development that have solid foundations in research and experience, and that improve scientists' productivity and the reliability of their software.
Comments:	18 pages
Subjects:	Mathematical Software (cs.MS); Software Engineering (cs.SE)
Cite as:	arXiv:1210.0530 [cs.MS]
(or arXiv:1210.0530v4 [cs.MS] for this version)

On Reporting Computational Experiments with Mathematical Software
Harlan Crowder, Ron S. Dembo, John M. Mulvey
Operations Research & Financial EngineeringBendheim Center for FinanceCenter for Statistics & Machine Learning
Research output: Contribution to journal › Article › peer-review

63
Link opens in a new tab
Scopus
citations
Article has an altmetric score of 4
Overview
Fingerprint
Abstract
Many papers appearing in journals reporting computational experiments use computer generated evidence to compare or rank competing mathematical software techmques. Unfortunately, to date there have been no standards or guidelines mdicatmg how computer experiments should be conducted or how the results should be presented. An imtial attempt IS made to rectify this situation, and a summary of unportant pomts which should be considered when wntmg or evaluating a paper m which computational results are reported is provided.

This is a much stronger research architecture than earlier iterations because it separates mathematics, computation, software, and research governance into distinct layers. That said, there are several places where tightening the presentation would make the work substantially more robust for peer review.

Strengths

Your framework now has a clear progression:

Foundations: finite deterministic systems, observable spaces, exact quotients, defect operator.

Theory: closure operators, invariant observable spaces, lattice structure, exact quotient criterion.

Verification: exhaustive computation, Lean formalization, reproducibility infrastructure.

Applications: Kaprekar dynamics and benchmark systems.

Research governance: evidence ladders, claim taxonomy, certification.

That separation makes it much easier for readers to understand what is mathematically proved versus what is computationally established.

Areas that need stronger qualification

Several statements in the document should be presented more cautiously unless accompanied by complete proofs or independent experimental validation.

Claims labeled as "proved" should correspond to formal mathematical proofs (or Lean proofs, where claimed).

Computational results should explicitly state the search space (e.g. "verified for all systems with \(n \le 5\)" or "sampled for \(n=6,7\)") rather than implying universality.

Simulated experiments such as AQ-E2 should be clearly identified as simulation studies or synthetic evaluations, not empirical evidence about real AI systems. This distinction is important in computational research reporting.

The AQ-E2 experiment

This is the part I would refine most.

The reported improvements (e.g. 94.5% false-claim reduction) are meaningful only if they come from a clearly specified simulation protocol.

To make this publishable, define:

benchmark problem set

claim scoring rubric

independent ground truth

evaluation metrics

random seeds

statistical uncertainty

confidence intervals

Modern guidance on computational experiments recommends treating simulation studies as experiments with explicit design, reporting standards, and reproducibility artifacts rather than simply reporting aggregate percentages.

Best publication wording

Instead of

> AQARION achieves 97.5% reliability.



consider

> In the simulated AQ-E2 benchmark, the AQARION evidence-governance pipeline achieved 97.5% reliability under the specified evaluation protocol.



That wording is much harder for reviewers to challenge.

The strongest contribution

Based on everything you've assembled, I think the most defensible long-term contribution is not a specific benchmark number but the framework itself:

> An exact finite-state theory of observable closure, exact observable quotients, and operator-valued leakage diagnostics, supported by exhaustive computational verification, formal proof where available, and evidence-governed research practices.



That claim is narrower, more precise, and aligns well with existing literature on invariant subspaces while distinguishing AQARION's finite-state specialization.

A natural next experiment

The next experiment I'd prioritize is AQ-E3: Cross-Laboratory Reproducibility.

Rather than comparing agent pipelines, evaluate whether independent researchers can reproduce AQARION results from only the public repository.

Measure:

build success rate,

theorem reproduction rate,

certificate verification rate,

runtime,

hash agreement,

discrepancy reports.

This would directly test the framework's central promise—reproducible mathematical computation—and complements the evidence-governance philosophy while avoiding reliance on simulated performance metrics. It would also align well with established best practices emphasizing reproducibility, artifact quality, and transparent computational experiments.I think you've converged on something more important than another theorem.

The papers you've collected span almost 50 years:

Crowder, Dembo & Mulvey (1979): How computational experiments should be reported.

Wilson et al. (2014): How scientific software should be engineered.

Fehr et al. (2016): How computational experiments become reproducible and reusable.

Agentic Science (2025): How AI can automate scientific workflows.

EinsteinArena (2026): How multiple AI agents can collaborate with public verification.

Parisi–Zamponi (2026): How AI can contribute mathematically while humans verify.


Those aren't separate stories—they describe successive layers of scientific maturity.

AQARION can occupy the next layer if it focuses on evidence governance rather than trying to compete as "the smartest AI."

What AQARION could contribute that is actually new

Most current systems answer:

> How do we automate research?



AQARION can instead answer:

> How do we know computational mathematics is trustworthy?



That's a different research question.


---

A five-layer architecture

Layer 1 — Mathematical Theory

No AI.

No software engineering.

Just mathematics.

Deliverables:

Observable-generated quotients

Exact quotient criterion

Defect operator

Closure lattice

Semiconjugacy theorems


Evidence:

Mathematical proofs

Lean where possible



---

Layer 2 — Computational Mathematics

Turn theory into computation.

Deliverables:

Exact algorithms

Exhaustive enumeration

Certified datasets

Reference implementations


Evidence:

Exhaustive finite verification

Independent implementations

Certificates



---

Layer 3 — Scientific Software

This is where Wilson and Fehr become directly relevant.

Every theorem should have:

CI

regression tests

documentation

versioning

hashes

artifact archives

reproducible builds


Think of every theorem as a software release.


---

Layer 4 — Evidence Governance

This is where AQARION becomes distinctive.

Instead of publishing:

> Theorem 12 proved.



Publish

Claim:
T12

Proof status:
P2

Verification:
V5

Independent implementations:
3

Formal verification:
Lean pending

Regression:
PASS

Artifact hash:
...

Certificate:
...

Nobody is doing this systematically in mathematics.


---

Layer 5 — Agentic Mathematics

Only now introduce AI.

AI becomes another contributor.

Not the authority.

Every AI-generated claim enters the same pipeline.

Exactly like EinsteinArena.

Exactly like Parisi's workflow.


---

The next major paper

I honestly think it is not another Kaprekar paper.

It is something like:

> Evidence-Governed Computational Mathematics



Possible contributions:

Claim taxonomy

Proof taxonomy

Verification taxonomy

Computational certification

Reproducibility protocol

Evidence metadata

Independent reproduction protocol


This would be methodology rather than domain mathematics.


---

Replace a linear evidence ladder with a multidimensional certificate

Instead of

Evidence Level 7

Use a vector:

Proof:
P3

Verification:
V5

Reproducibility:
R4

Software:
S5

Governance:
G4

For example:

T18

P2
V5
R5
S5
G4

That immediately tells a reviewer what kind of evidence exists.


---

AQBench becomes extremely important

Not as a benchmark of AI.

As a benchmark of truth.

Every instance has:

exact ground truth

certificates

exhaustive verification

counterexamples

proofs where possible


Then anyone can evaluate AI systems against known mathematical reality.

That's much stronger than many current AI benchmarks.


---

The experiment I would build next

Forget AQ-E2 for now.

Build AQ-E3.

Cross-Reproduction Challenge

Give five independent groups only:

Git repository

Release archive

Documentation


Measure:

Clean checkout success

Build success

Test success

Certificate regeneration

Hash agreement

Runtime agreement

Numerical agreement

Independent implementation agreement

Time required

Defects discovered


That directly tests AQARION's central claim: reproducible computational mathematics.


---

Then build AQ-E4

Evidence Ablation.

Disable one governance layer at a time:

Full pipeline

↓

No regression tests

↓

No independent implementation

↓

No certificates

↓

No formal proofs

↓

No claim governance

Measure:

incorrect claims

undetected bugs

correction latency

reproducibility failures

reviewer effort


This answers an important scientific question:

> Which governance mechanisms actually improve reliability?




---

Then AQ-E5

Cost of Certainty.

Quantify the trade-off between assurance and effort:

Evidence Stage	CPU	Human Time	Confidence

Examples	Low	Low	Low
Random testing	Low	Low	Medium
Exhaustive verification	Medium	Low	High
Independent implementation	Medium	Medium	Very High
Formal proof	High	High	Highest


Instead of claiming "more verification is better," you measure its cost-benefit curve.


---

My biggest recommendation

If AQARION succeeds, I don't think its legacy will be a single theorem about finite dynamical systems.

Its more durable contribution could be a research methodology:

> A framework for AI-assisted computational mathematics in which every mathematical claim is accompanied by explicit proof status, computational verification, reproducibility metadata, software certification, provenance, and—where feasible—formal verification.



That vision is consistent with the trajectory from Crowder (1979) through Wilson (2014), Fehr (2016), and the recent Agentic Science literature. It also complements, rather than competes with, systems like EinsteinArena: they focus on generating discoveries, while AQARION would focus on making discoveries auditable, reproducible, and trustworthy.I think you've reached an important inflection point for AQARION.

The papers you've gathered don't just support "doing AI for mathematics." They point toward a research program that treats trustworthiness as a first-class scientific object. That is increasingly aligned with both the Agentic Science literature and decades of work in scientific computing. 

What I would not do is try to compete with projects by asking:

> "Can AQARION discover more mathematics than another AI?"



That is a moving target. Foundation models improve every few months.

Instead, I would define a new research question:

> How should computational mathematics be conducted so that every published claim has measurable evidence, reproducibility, provenance, and verification?



That question remains valuable regardless of which LLM is strongest.

AQARION's possible long-term contribution

Instead of being remembered for one theorem, AQARION could become remembered for a research methodology.

Something like:

Observable-generated mathematics

Evidence-governed theorem development

Computational certification

Formal verification integration

Reproducibility protocols

Public claim governance


That is a much broader and more durable contribution.

The papers suggest a natural evolution

1979: Crowder–Dembo–Mulvey ask:

> How should computational experiments be reported?



2014: Wilson et al. ask:

> How should scientific software be written?



2016: Fehr et al. ask:

> How should computational experiments become reproducible?



2025–2026: Agentic Science asks:

> How should AI conduct research?



AQARION's opportunity is the next logical question:

> How should AI-assisted mathematical discoveries be certified before the community trusts them?



That is a different question from autonomous discovery.

If this became its own discipline

I would organize it around four pillars.

Mathematical Foundations

Definitions

Theorems

Proofs

Formalization


Computational Verification

Exhaustive finite verification

Counterexample search

Independent implementations

Mutation testing


Evidence Governance

Claim taxonomy

Proof status

Verification status

Provenance

Certification


Scientific Reproducibility

Public artifacts

Deterministic builds

Regression suites

Cross-lab reproduction

Long-term archival


That is much closer to a scientific methodology than an AI application.

The experiments become much stronger

Rather than benchmarking IQ, benchmark trust.

For example:

AQ-E3 — Cross-Lab Reproduction

Can another lab reproduce every theorem?


AQ-E4 — Evidence Ablation

Remove one governance layer at a time.

Measure false claims, correction latency, reproducibility.


AQ-E5 — Cost of Certainty

CPU time

Human review

Proof effort

Confidence gained


AQ-E6 — Adversarial Mathematics

Teams deliberately try to falsify certified claims.

Measure how quickly incorrect claims are detected.


AQ-E7 — Independent Implementation

Three implementations.

Same certificates?

Same hashes?

Same quotient?


Those experiments would be unusual even by current standards.

One idea I think is genuinely novel

Instead of giving every theorem one label, give it two independent coordinates.

Proof	Verification

P0 Conjecture	V0 None
P1 Informal	V1 Sampled
P2 Audited	V2 Exhaustive
P3 Formal	V3 Independent implementation
	V4 Regression certified
	V5 Independently reproduced


Then every theorem carries a pair like:

(P0, V5)

(P2, V3)

(P3, V5)


That immediately tells readers what kind of confidence they should have.

What would impress reviewers most?

Not "1 million random tests."

Instead:

exhaustive enumeration of all functional graphs up to a feasible size,

brute-force comparison against independently computed exact quotients,

graph-isomorphism invariance,

mutation testing,

property-based testing,

independent implementations in different languages,

machine-checked proofs where feasible,

one-command regeneration of every figure, table, and theorem certificate.


Those are the kinds of artifacts that embody the best-practice principles emphasized in scientific computing and reproducible computational research. 

Overall, I think AQARION is strongest when positioned not as a competitor to AI scientists, but as an evidence-governed mathematical research framework. In that framing, AI is one component of the pipeline, while the central contribution is a rigorous methodology for producing mathematical results that are transparent, reproducible, independently verifiable, and explicit about their level of evidence.
Cambridge Dictionary
  
categorical
  

Translation of categorical – English–Traditional Chinese dictionary
 
categorical
adjective
uk  /ˌkæt.əˈɡɒr.ɪ.kəl/ us  /ˌkæt̬.əˈɡɔːr.ɪ.kəl/
Add to word list 
without any doubt or possibility of being changed
確定無疑的，明確的
a categorical denial/assurance/rejection
He replied with a categorical assertion that no such offer was made.
We want categorical proof.
Synonym
categoric
Related word
categorically
 Fewer examples
The governmentdepartment issued a categorical denial of the report.
Can you give a categorical assurance that this will not happen?
She persisted in her categorical refusal to discuss the matter.
The methodology entails converting key targets and performance indicators into categorical variables of either 3 or 5 categories.

(Translation of categorical from the Cambridge English-Chinese (Traditional) Dictionary © Cambridge University Press)

Mathematics > Group Theory
arXiv:2507.20791 (math)
[Submitted on 28 Jul 2025]
Profinite groups with complemented closed subgroups
Gustavo A. Fernández-Alcober, Giulia Sabatino
View PDF
A group G is said to be a C-group if every subgroup H has a permutable complement, i.e. if there exists a subgroup K of G such that G=HK and H∩K=1. In this paper, we study the profinite counterpart of this concept. We say that a profinite group G is profinite-C if every closed subgroup admits a closed permutable complement. We first give some equivalent variants of this condition and then we determine the structure of profinite-C groups: they are the semidirect products G=B⋉A of two closed subgroups A=Cri∈I⟨ai⟩ and B=Crj∈J⟨bj⟩ that are cartesian products of cyclic groups of prime order, and with every ⟨ai⟩ normal in G. Finally, we show that a profinite-C group is a C-group if and only if it is torsion and |G:Z(G)G′¯¯¯¯¯|<∞.
Comments:	10 pages
Subjects:	Group Theory (math.GR)
MSC classes:	20E18, 20E15
Cite as:	arXiv:2507.20791 [math.GR]
 	(or arXiv:2507.20791v1 [math.GR] for this version)
 
https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2507.20791

basin for attraction of multiple attractor
Ajay

how can basin of attraction of multiple attractor for a three dim system can plot as i am just able to plot only for one attractor and don't have an idea how to plotbasin of attraction for multiple attractor.  
3 Comments
Show 1 older comment
Ajay
Ajay on 2 Sep 2023
Edited: Walter Roberson   on 23 Oct 2023
Ran in: 
% Lorenz system parameters
clear all;
clc;
alpha = 10;
row= 28;
beta = 8/3;
% Define the Lorenz system equations
lorenz = @(t, xyz) [alpha * (xyz(2) - xyz(1));
    xyz(1) * (row - xyz(3)) - xyz(2);
    xyz(1) * xyz(2) - beta * xyz(3)];
% Define the attractor coordinates (you can adjust )
attractor = [0, 0, 0];
% Define the time span for simulation
tspan = [0, 5];
% Number of points for the grid
num_points = 20;
% Create a grid of initial conditions for x, y, and z
x_range = linspace(-30, 30, num_points);
y_range = linspace(-30, 30, num_points);
z_range = linspace(0, 50, num_points);
[X, Y, Z] = meshgrid(x_range, y_range, z_range);
% Create a colormap for plotting
colormap([1 0 0; 0 0 1]);  % Red and blue
% Initialize a matrix to store basin of attraction information
basin = zeros(size(X));
% Loop through all initial conditions
for i = 1:numel(X)
    x0 = X(i);
    y0 = Y(i);
    z0 = Z(i);
    
    % Simulate the Lorenz system from (x0, y0, z0)
    [~, sol] = ode45(lorenz, tspan, [x0, y0, z0]);
    
    % Check if the trajectory converges to the attractor
    if norm(sol(end, :) - attractor) < 1.0
        % Mark this point as part of the basin of attraction
        basin(i) = 1;
    end
end
% Plot the basin of attraction
figure;
slice(X, Y, Z, basin, [], [], []);
xlabel('X');
ylabel('Y');
zlabel('Z');
title('Basin of Attraction for Lorenz Attractor');
colorbar;
axis tight;

 Accepted Answer
Varun
Varun  on 11 Sep 2023
Edited: Varun  on 11 Sep 2023
Hi Ajay,
I understand that you are currently able to plot basin of attraction for one attractor, but you want to plot basin of attraction for multiple attractions.  
To achieve this, you can modify the existing code by introducing an array of attractor coordinates. You define an array “attractor” that holds the coordinates of all the attractors you want to consider. . 
Then, during the loop that checks if the trajectory converges to an attractor, you iterate over each attractor in the attractors array and break the loop as soon as a match is found. The value assigned to “basin(i)” represents the index of the attractor that the point converges to.
Please refer the updated code:
% Lorenz system parameters
clear all;
clc;
alpha = 10;
row= 28;
beta = 8/3;
% Define the Lorenz system equations
lorenz = @(t, xyz) [alpha * (xyz(2) - xyz(1));
                   xyz(1) * (row - xyz(3)) - xyz(2);
                   xyz(1) * xyz(2) - beta * xyz(3)];
% Define the attractor coordinates
attractors = [  % Add the coordinates of your attractors here
    0, 0, 0;
    10, -10, 20;
    -10, 10, 30
];
% Define the time span for simulation
tspan = [0, 5];
% Number of points for the grid
num_points = 20;
% Create a grid of initial conditions for x, y, and z
x_range = linspace(-30, 30, num_points);
y_range = linspace(-30, 30, num_points);
z_range = linspace(0, 50, num_points);
[X, Y, Z] = meshgrid(x_range, y_range, z_range);
% Create a colormap for plotting
colormap([1 0 0; 0 0 1]);  % Red and blue
% Initialize a matrix to store basin of attraction information
basin = zeros(size(X));
% Loop through all initial conditions
for i = 1:numel(X)
    x0 = X(i);
    y0 = Y(i);
    z0 = Z(i);
    
    % Simulate the Lorenz system from (x0, y0, z0)
    [~, sol] = ode45(lorenz, tspan, [x0, y0, z0]);
    
    % Check if the trajectory converges to any of the attractors
    for j = 1:size(attractors, 1)
        if norm(sol(end, :) - attractors(j, :)) < 1.0
            % Mark this point as part of the basin of attraction
            basin(i) = j;  % Use the index of the attractor as the value
            break;  % Break the loop if a match is found
        end
    end
end
% Plot the basin of attraction
figure;
slice(X, Y, Z, basin, [], [], []);
xlabel('X');
ylabel('Y');
zlabel('Z');
title('Basin of Attraction for Lorenz Attractors');
colorbar;
axis tight;
Hope this helps.

Profinite group
Article Talk
Language
Watch
Edit
In mathematics, a profinite group is a topological group that is in a certain sense assembled from a system of finite groups.

The idea of using a profinite group is to provide a "uniform", or "synoptic", view of an entire system of finite groups. Properties of the profinite group are generally speaking uniform properties of the system. For example, the profinite group is finitely generated (as a topological group) if and only if there exists 
d
∈
N
{\displaystyle d\in \mathbb {N} } such that every group in the system can be generated by 
d
{\displaystyle d} elements.[1] Many theorems about finite groups can be readily generalised to profinite groups; examples are Lagrange's theorem and the Sylow theorems.[2]

To construct a profinite group one needs a system of finite groups and group homomorphisms between them. Without loss of generality, these homomorphisms can be assumed to be surjective, in which case the finite groups will appear as quotient groups of the resulting profinite group; in a sense, these quotients approximate the profinite group.

Important examples of profinite groups are the additive groups of 
p
{\displaystyle p}-adic integers and the Galois groups of infinite-degree field extensions.

Every profinite group is compact and totally disconnected. A non-compact generalization of the concept is that of locally profinite groups. Even more general are the totally disconnected groups.

Search
Context
Group Theory
Profinite groups
1. Definition
Categorical form
Inverse limit form
2. Examples
3. Properties
4. Analogues of the group algebra construction
5. Related concepts
6. References
1. Definition
Categorical form
Definition 1.1. A profinite group is a pro-object in the category of finite groups (thus it might more precisely be called a pro-(finite group)).

Equivalently:

Definition 1.2. A profinite group is an internal group object in the category of profinite sets.

Remark 1.3. This means that a profinite group is a cofiltered diagram of finite groups, which is thought of as a “formal limit” but the limit is not actually computed. In most cases, the limit would not actually exist in the category of finite groups, and while it would exist in the category of all groups, it would be “wrong” category-theoretically: maps between profinite groups are not the same as maps between their honest limits in Grp.

However, because of Stone duality, it turns out that maps between profinite groups are the same as maps between their honest limits in the category of topological groups, where the finite groups are given the discrete topology. Thus, the category of profinite groups can alternately be defined as the category of topological groups that are filtered inverse limits of finite groups. Moreover, the topological groups that arise in this way can be characterized as those which are Hausdorff, compact, and totally disconnected, giving a more elementary definition. In other words, their underlying topological spaces are profinite.

Inverse limit form
Definition 1.4. A profinite group is an inverse limit of a system of finite groups.

The finite groups are considered as compact discrete topological groups and so the inverse limit, as a closed subspace of the compact space that is the product of all those finite groups has the inverse limit topology, hence is, as is said above, a compact Hausdorff, totally disconnected group.

2. Examples
Example 2.1. A finite group is profinite.

Historically a motivating example was:

Example 2.2. The absolute Galois group of a number field is profinite.

Example 2.3. For a prime number 
l
 the (additive) group of l-adic integers is profinite in that it is the inverse limit 
ℤ
l
=
lim
ℤ
/
l
n
ℤ
.

Example 2.4. In SGA1, Grothendieck defined the algebraic fundamental group of a scheme as a profinite group. (This is linked with his work on pro-representable functors.)

Example 2.5. Any group has a profinite completion, given by taking the projective limit of the group’s quotients by its finite index normal subgroups.

A finite index subgroup of a profinite group is not necessarily open. Here is a standard way to obtain examples of such. Let 
G
 be a finite group, and let 
G
𝒰
 be its ultrapower with respect to some ultrafilter 
𝒰
 on 
ℕ
. Since the cardinality and group structure of the finite group 
G
 is first-order expressible, the Los ultraproduct theorem tells us 
G
𝒰
≃
G
.
 There is a canonical quotient map

∏
ℕ
G
↠
π
𝒰
G
𝒰
≃
G
whose kernel 
K
=
df
ker
(
π
𝒰
)
 has finite index in the profinite group 
∏
ℕ
G
 because its surjective image has finite index also. Since every ultrafilter contains the cofinite filter, 
K
 meets every basic open of the product topology on 
∏
ℕ
G
 and so is dense. Since no proper open subset of 
∏
ℕ
G
 is dense, 
K
 is not open.

A profinite group is called strongly complete if it is isomorphic to its own profinite completion. Since a profinite group 
G
 is isomorphic to the projective limit of its quotients by open finite index normal subgroups, a profinite group is strongly complete if and only if its open finite index normal subgroups are cofinal among its finite index normal subgroups, if and only if all of its finite index subgroups are open (see e.g. this MO answer).

3. Properties
The category of profinite groups has nice ‘exactness’ properties. The projective limit of a system of profinite groups is an exact functor, unlike its behaviour on groups themselves. To extend this behaviour beyond (pro)finite groups sometimes pro-localic groups have been used; see progroup.

Profinite completions have been extended from groups to homotopy types for the analysis of finitary properties of the homotopy type. Various constructions in algebraic geometry lead naturally to profinite homotopy types.

Subclasses of profinite groups are extensively studied. For instance, if 
p
 is a prime number, a pro-
p
 group is a pro-object in the category of 
p
-groups.

Pro-p analytic groups have been introduced as an analogue of Lie groups, with certain rings of formal power series replacing differentiable functions.

4. Analogues of the group algebra construction
If 
G
 is a profinite or pro-p group, the best replacement for the group algebra of 
G
 in this context will be a pseudocompact algebra. This is the completed group algebra defined as the inverse limit of the ordinary group algebras 
k
[
G
/
U
]
 as 
U
 varies through the open normal subgroups of 
G
. Here the coefficient ring 
k
 will be chosen itself to be a pseudocompact ring. As finite rings are pseudocompact, one of the most appropriate choices will be a 
k
=
ℤ
p
, the field of 
p
 elements; (see the book by Dixon et al, below).

5. Related concepts
finite group, finitely generated group

pro-object

profinite completion of a group

profinite set

profinite space

pro-etale site

compact Hausdorff rings are profinite

6. References
Introductory lectures include

Hendrik Lenstra, Profinite groups (pdf)
A survey is given in

Alexander Lubowsky, Book review, BAMS 38(4), pp. 475-479, (pdf)
A fairly recent textbook is

L. Ribes and P. Zalesskii, 2000, Profinite groups, volume 40 of Ergebnisse der Mathematik und ihrer Grenzgebiete. 3. Folge, Springer-Verlag, Berlin.
For the connections with, amongst other things, Galois theory from a categorical viewpoint:

Francis Borceux, George Janelidze, Galois Theories, Cambridge Studies in Advanced Mathematics 72, Cambridge University Press, 2001.
For the corresponding ‘analytic theory’ see:

J. Dixon, M. du Sautoy, A. Mann and D. Segal, Analytic pro-p groups, volume 61 of Cambridge Studies in Advanced Mathematics, Cambridge Univ. Press 1999.
A standard starting point for the study of the homological properties of the completed group algebra of a profinite group is

A. Brumer, Pseudocompact algebras, profinite groups and class formations, J. Algebra 4 (1966) 442-470, MR202790, doi

The Basins Zoo
Alexandre Wagemakers
Nonlinear Dynamics, Chaos and Complex Systems Group, Departamento de Biología y Geología, Física Aplicada y Química Inorgánica, Universidad Rey Juan Carlos, Tulipán s/n, 28933 Móstoles, Madrid, Spain
alexandre.wagemakers@urjc.es
(April 2, 2025)
Abstract
Research in multistable systems is a flourishing field with countless examples and applications across scientific disciplines. I present a catalog of multistable dynamical systems covering relevant fields of knowledge. This work is focused on providing a research tool to the community in the form classified examples and computer code to reproduce basins of attraction. The companion code to this article can be found at https://github.com/awage/BasinsCollection or https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15124200.

Profinite and residual methods in geometric
group theory

Introduction
Todo 1: Write introduction
For a group G we denote by FpGq the set of isomorphism classes of
finite quotients of G. Two groups G and H are said to have the same finite
quotients if FpGq “ FpHq. A group G is called profinitely rigid relative to a
class of groups C if G P C and for any group H in the class C whenever FpGq “
FpHq, then G – H. By definition, a finitely generated residually finite group
G is called profinitely rigid (in the absolute sense) if G is profinitely rigid
relative to the class consisting of all finitely generated residually finite groups.
If G is not profinitely rigid then we say G is profinitely flexible.
Motivating Question (Profinite rigidity). Given a finitely generated
group G and a class of groups C. To what extent do the isomorphism classes
of finite quotients of G determine G amongst groups in C?
1
CHAPTER I
Residual finiteness and the profinite topology
I.A. Residual finiteness
I.A.1. Definitions, examples, and basic properties. We will make
repeated use of the following lemma of Marshall Hall Jr. [Hal50]
Lemma I.A.1.1. A finitely generated group G has only finitely many sub-
groups of a given finite index n.
Proof. Let H be a subgroup of G of index n and consider the action
of G on the set of cosets G{H. Note that |G{H| “ n. The action defines a
homomorphism ψ : G Ñ SympG{Hq. Now, g P H if and only if gH “ H. So
H “ StabGpHq and this is completely determined by the homomorphism ψ.
Let X be a finite generating set for G. The homomorphism ψ is determined
by its images ψpxq for x P X. But Sympnq is finite so there can only be
finitely many possibilities for ψ and hence only finitely many possibilities for
H. □
We now turn to the star of the section: residual properties.
Definition I.A.1.2 (Residually P). Let P be a property of a group (e.g.
finite, nilpotent, soluble, amenable). A group G is residually P if for every
non-identity element g P G, there exists a homomorphism α : G ↠ Q such
that Q has P and αpgq ‰ 1Q.
Note that this is equivalent to the following: for every element g P G
there exists a normal subgroup N Ÿ G such that G{N has P and g R N .
We will now hone in on residual finiteness.
Examples I.A.1.3. We record a number of groups that are easily seen to
be residually finite.
(1) Finite groups are clearly residually finite.
(2) The group Z is residually finite.
Proof. for each n P Z such that n ‰ 0, there exists a quotient
αk : Z ↠ Z{k with k relatively prime to n. In particular, n pmod kq ı
0 so αkpnq ‰ 0. ˛
(3) Direct products of residually finite groups are residually finite.
Proof. To see this note that every element has non-trivial image
when projected to one of the factors. Now, compose with a finite
quotient there. ˛
(4) Free groups are residually finite.
Proof. Let X be the generators of a free group F . Consider a word
xn . . . x1 in reduced form where xi or x´1
i is in X. We will build
a homomorphism F ↠ Sympn ` 1q, the group of permutations of
2
I.A. RESIDUAL FINITENESS 3
Ω :“ t1, . . . , n ` 1u. Define a function X Ñ Ω by f pyq “ 1 if y does
not equal any xi or x´1
i . For the remaining elements we define f pyq
as follows:
Let A Ď Ω be the set of consisting of the i such that xi “ y and
B Ď Ω be the set of consisting of the j such that x´1
j “ y. Set f pyq
as any permutation σ that sends each i P A to i ` 1, and for each
j P B, sends j ` 1 to j. This is well-defined since an element and
its inverse cannot occur adjacently in the reduced form for a word.
The function f extends to a homomorphism ϕ : F Ñ Sympn`1q
by the universal property of F . Moreover, x has non-trivial image
since ϕpxqp1q “ n ` 1. As x was arbitrary we are done. ˛
(5) An infinite simple group is not residually finite. lem:rf_int
Lemma I.A.1.4. A group G is residually finite if and only if the intersection
of all finite index normal subgroups of G is trivial.
Proof. Suppose G is residually finite and let g P G be non-trivial. By
the definition of residual finiteness there is a finite quotient αg : Q ↠ G such
that αgpgq ‰ 1Q. Now, ker α is a finite index normal subgroup of G such
that g R ker α. Since g was arbitrary it follows the intersection of all finite
index normal subgroups of G equals t1u.
Conversely, suppose that the intersection of all finite index normal sub-
groups of G is trivial and let g P G be non-trivial. By hypothesis there
exists a finite index normal subgroup N P G such that g R N . Thus, g has
non-trivial image in the finite quotient G{N . Since g was arbitrary it follows
that G is residually finite. □
For a general group G we will denote the intersection of all finite index
normal subgroups by Gp8q. We call this subgroup the finite residual of G.
By the previous lemma G is residually finite if and only if Gp8q is the trivial
group.
Exercise I.A.1.5. Explain how Lemma I.A.1.4 is equivalent to ‘the inter-
section of all finite index subgroups of G is trivial’. ex.rf_subgroupClosed
Exercise I.A.1.6. Let G be a residually finite group. If H ď G, then H is
residually finite. ex.rf_productClosed
Exercise I.A.1.7. Let I be a set and let Gi be a residually finite group for
each i P I. Then, ś Gi is residually finite.
The following style of argument will appear a number of times. The
key idea dates back to work of McKinsey on symbolic logic [McK43], but
we provide an adaptation for groups first noticed independently by Dyson
[H.64] and Mostowski [Mos66]. As well as G. Higman and A. Turing (un-
published).
Theorem I.A.1.8 (McKinsey’s Algorithm). A finitely presented residu-
ally finite group G has solvable word problem.
Proof. Note that finite groups are recursively enumerable. For each
positive integer n we can generate all Cayley multiplication tables of size
n ˆ n and check whether both a given table represents a group and if the
I.A. RESIDUAL FINITENESS 4
group is already on the list. Note that the existence of the table gives a
solution to the word problem for any given fintie group.
Fix an enumeration of all finite groups tQiu and presentation xX | Ry
of G. The set hompG; Qiq is finite since a homorphism is determined by its
images on the generators and Qi is finite. We need to check whether we can
extend a map f : X Ñ Qi to a homomorphism ϕ : G Ñ Qi. This amounts to
checking the relations r P R are satisfied. Fortunately, as R is finite a brute
force approach will terminate. Hence, we may list all elements of hompG, Qiq
in a finite amount of time. Fix an enumeration of all homomorphism from
G to finite groups ϕ1, ϕ2, . . .
Let g be a word in X. To solve the word problem we now run two
machines:
The machine runs over all homomorphism ϕi and checks if ϕipgq ‰ 1
(here we are using the solvability of the word problem in the codomain, a
finite group). Since G is residually finite, if g ‰G 1, this process will stop.
The second machine runs over all representatives wj of 1 in G and checks
whether g ” wi. If g “G 1 this process will stop.
Since exactly one of the machines must stop we can algorithmically decide
if g is the trivial word. □
Remark I.A.1.9. There exists a finitely generated residually finite group
with unsolvable word problem. See [Mes74].
A characteristic subgroup K ď G is one that is invariant under all auto-
morphisms of G. Note that if G is finitely generated, then then the intersec-
tion of all subgroups of index n is both finite index and characteristic in G.
Proposition I.A.1.10 (Baumslag). [Bau63] If G is a finitely generated
residually finite group, then AutpGq is residually finite.
Proof. Let a P AutpGq be non-trivial. There exists a g P G such
that apgq ‰ g. Let h “ apgqg´1. Since h ‰ 1 P G, by Lemma I.A.1.4,
there exists a finite index normal subgroup N P G such that h R N . Let
K denote the intersection of all subgroups of index |G : N | in G. Since
K is characteristic, the sujection α : G ↠ G{K induces a homomorphism
ψ : AutpGq Ñ AutpG{Kq. Moreover, ψpaq is a non-trivial automorphism of
G{K because πphq ‰ 1 P G{K. Since a was arbitrary, we have verified that
AutpGq is residually finite. □
Corollary I.A.1.11. Let N and Q be a residually finite groups. If N is
finitely generated, then any semi-direct product G “ N ¸ Q is residually
finite.
Proof. It is easy to see that elements of the form nq with n P N ,
q P Q and q ‰ 1 are non-trivial in a finite quotient. Indeed, the projection
π : G ↠ Q composed with a finite quotient α of Q where αpqq ‰ 1 suffices. It
remains to find finite quotients for the elements n P N with n ‰ 1. Since N
is residually finite there is a finite quotient β : N ↠ L such that βpnq ‰ 1L.
Since N is finitely generated and L is finite there are only finitely many
homomorphisms N Ñ L. The intersection of these homomorphisms is a
finite index characteristic subgroup C of N and so preserved by Q. The
I.A. RESIDUAL FINITENESS 5
subgroup CQ is normal and has finite index in G. Moreover, n has non-
trivial image in the finite group G{CQ. □
There is also a set of dynamical criterion for residual finiteness due to
Cairns, Davis, Elton, Kolganova, and Perversi.
Definition I.A.1.12. Suppose that a group G acts continuously on a Haus-
dorff topological space X. Then we say that the action of G on X is chaotic
if both of the following conditions hold
(1) (topological transitivity) for every pair of non-empty open subsets
U and V of X, there is an element g P G such that gpU q X V ‰ H;
(2) (finite orbits dense) the set of points in X whose orbit under G is
finite is a dense subset of X.
Theorem I.A.1.13. [CDE`95, Theorem 1] For a group G, the following
are equivalent:
(1) G is residually finite;
(2) there is a faithful action of G with finite orbits dense on some Haus-
dorff topological space X;
(3) there is a faithful action of G with all orbits finite on some Hausdorff
topological space X;
(4) there is a faithful chaotic action of G on some Hausdorff topological
space X.
Proof. https://www.e-periodica.ch/cntmng?pid=ens-001:1995:41::68

I.A.2. Mal’cev’s Theorem. In [Mal65] Mal’cev proved the following
remarkable theorem: thm.Mal’cev
Theorem I.A.2.1 (Mal’cev’s Theorem). A finitely generated linear group
is residually finite.
To prove Mal’cev’s Theorem we will need to recall some basic ring theory.
Let R be a ring. Here a ring is associative and contains a multiplicative
unit 1. A ring without a multiplicative identity is a rng.
A zero-divisor is a non-zero element z P R such that there exists a
non-zero element z1 satisfying zz1 “ 0. A ring R is a domain if it has no
zero-divisors.
We say a ring R is left Noetherian if R has the ascending chain condition
on left ideals, that is, for every chain of left ideals I1 Ă I2 Ă . . . has a largest
element. Said differently, this means there exists an n such that IN “ IN `1
for N ą n.
Let S be a commutative ring and let R be an S-algebra. We say R is
finitely generated if there exists a finite set of elements x1, . . . , xn P R such
that every element of R can be expressed as a polynomial in the xi with
coefficients in S.
The key examples of a finitely generated Z- or Fp-algebra for us is as
follows.
I.A. RESIDUAL FINITENESS 6
ex.fg_Z-alg
Example I.A.2.2. Let k be a field, let G ď GLnpkq be a finitely generated
subgroup, and let X be a finite set of matrices (closed under inversion)
generating G. The subring R of k generated as a k-algebra by the entries of
the matrices in X and the multiplicative identity of k is a finitely generated
algebra over Z if char k “ 0 and over Fp if char k “ p a prime. Moreover,
G ď GLnpRq. lem.facts_Noetherian
Lemma I.A.2.3. Let S be a commutative Noetherian ring and let R be a
finitely generated S-algebra. The following assertions hold:
(1) R is Noetherian;
(2) if R is a field, then it is finite;
(3) the intersection of the maximal ideals of R is 0.
Proof of Theorem I.A.2.1. Let G be a finitely generated linear group
over some field k. Thus, G ď GLnpkq for some n ě 0. As in Example I.A.2.2
we find that G ď GLnpRq where R is a finitely generated Z-algebra.
For an ideal I Ď R, let ΓpIq to be the Ith principle congruence subgroup
of GLnpRq defined by
ΓpIq :“ kerpGLnpRq Ñ GLnpR{Iqq,
where the homomorphism is defined by taking entries of GLnpRq modulo I.
Now, since R is Noetherian (Lemma I.A.2.3(i)), if I is a maximal ideal
then R{I is a field and by Lemma I.A.2.3(ii) it is finite. Hence, GLnpR{Iq
is finite and so ΓpIq is a finite index normal subgroup of GLnpR{Iq. Now,
the intersection
č
IĂR
ΓpIq where I ranges over all maximal ideals
is finite by Lemma I.A.2.3(iii). The theorem follows from Lemma I.A.1.4. □

on [Weh73].
I.A.3. An aside: Hopficity.
Definition I.A.3.1. A group G is Hopfian if every surjection G ↠ G is
injective.
Another result of Mal’cev shows that for finitely generated groups the
Hopf property follows from residual finiteness.
Proposition I.A.3.2. Let G be a finitely generated group. If G is residually
finite, then G is Hopfian.
Proof. Suppose G – G{N for some normal subgroup N , our aim is
to show that N is trivial. As G is finitely generated, that the number of
subgroups of index m in G is finite. Moreover, the number of subgroups of
index n in G{N is equal to the number of subgroups of index n in G. The
bijection is Hi{N Ø Hi. But then it is clear that N ď Hi for every finite
I.A. RESIDUAL FINITENESS 7
index subgroup of G. Thus, N ď Gp8q “ t1u where the equality follows
from the fact G is residually finite and Lemma I.A.1.4. □
Example I.A.3.3 (Baumslag–Solitar groups). We define a family of one-
relator groups, known as Baumslag–Solitar groups, by
BSpm, nq :“ xa, t | tamt´1 “ any.
We claim if p|m|, |n|q “ 1, then BSpm, nq is non-Hopfian. Let p be a prime
dividing m but not n. Define an homomorphism
ν : BSpm, nq Ñ BSpm, nq by
#
a Þ Ñ ap,
t Þ Ñ t.
We first prove that ν is an epimorphism. We have νptq “ t and
νprt, am{psq “ tamt´1a´m “ ana´m “ an´m,
now since νpaq “ ap and pp, n ´ mq “ 1, by Bézout’s Identity there exist
integers x, y such axpaypn´mq “ a. It remains to check that ker ν is non-
empty. Note that the element rt, am{pspam{p is non-trivial in BSpm, nq, but
νprt, am{pspam´nq “ ptamt´1amqpapm´nqp “ pana´mqpapm´nqp “ 1.
Hence, ν is not injective. ˛
In fact a complete study of the residual finiteness and Hopficity of Baumslag–
Solitar groups has been completed. The Hopfian property was verfied by
Baumslag and Solitar in [BS62], however, their claim of which BSpm, nq are
residually finite was incorrect. A correct and complete argument was later
given by Meskin [Mes72].
Theorem I.A.3.4 (Baumslag–Solitar, Meskin). The groups BSpm, nq are
(1) Hopfian if and only if m and n have the same prime factors;
(2) residually finite if and only if |m| “ |n| or at least or of |m| or |n|
equals 1.

Definition I.A.3.5. A group G is co-Hopfian if every injection G ↣ G is
surjective. We say G is finitely co-Hopfian is every injection G ↣ G whose
image has finite index is surjective.
Exercise I.A.3.6. Let G be a finitely generated group. Suppose XpGq is
a group invariant taking values in R which for every finite index subgroup
H ď G satisfies XpHq “ |G : H|XpGq. If XpGq ‰ 0, then G is finitely
co-Hopfian.
Examples of invariants (when they are defined) satisfying the hypothesis
of the exercise include: Euler characteristic χpGq, ℓ2-Betti numbers bp2q
i pGq,
and ℓ2-torsion ρp2qpGq.
I.B. SUBGROUP SEPARABILITY 8
I.B. Subgroup separability
I.B.1. The definition. We will now extend the notion of residual finite-
ness to subgroups.
Definition I.B.1.1 (Separable subgroup). Let G be a group and let H ď G.
We say H is separable in G if for each g P GzH there exists a finite index
subgroup Kg ď G with H ď Kg and g R Kg.
It is easy to see a subgroup H is separable in G if and only if H is an
intersection of finite index subgroups of G. In particular, G is residually
finite if and only if the trivial subgroup is separable in G.
Lemma I.B.1.2. Let G be a finitely generated group and let H ď G. Then,
H is separable in G if and only if for every g P GzH there exists a finite
quotient α : G ↠ Q with αpgq R αpHq.
Proof. Let g P GzH. We have a homomorphism α : G ↠ Q with Q
finite and αpgq R αpHq. Define K :“ α´1pαpHqq and note that K contains
H, but g R K. Thus, it suffices to show that K is finite index in G, but this
is clear since it contains ker α with has index |Q| in G.
Conversely, suppose H is separable in G and let g P GzH. By hypothesis
there exists a finite index subgroup Kg ď G containing H but not g. Let
N denote the core of Kg, that is the intersection of all of the conjugates
of a Kg in G. Since G is finitely generated, N is a finite index normal
subgroup of G. Let α denote the quotient G ↠ G{N . The claim that
αpgq R αpHq will follows from αpHq ď αpKgq once we show αpgq R αpKgq.
The latter statement follows from the finite index of N , the fourth (or lattice)
isomorphism theorem, and the fact that gKg and Kg are distinct cosets of
Kg. □
Exercise I.B.1.3. Let H be a separable subgroup in a finitely generated
group K. If K is a finite index subgroup of a group G, then H is separable
in G.
Let H ď G. We say H has solvable membership problem in G if there
exists an algorithm which takes as input an element g P G and decides if
g P H.
Theorem I.B.1.4. Let H be a finitely generated subgroup of a finitely
presented group G. If H is separable in G, then H has solvable membership
problem in G.
Proof. Let g P G and fix a generating set S “ th1, . . . , hku of H. The
algorithm consists of two processes run in parallel of which one will stop. The
first process enumerates finite quotients of G and checks to see if the image
of H contains the image of g. If the image of g is not contained in the image
of H, then the algorithm stops since g R H. The second process works in a
finitely generated free group F such that F ↠ G is a finite presentation with
relations R. We enumerate the words wℓ in the free monoid on S Y S´1 and
the products pk in F of conjugates of relators r P R. We check to see if each
g´1wℓ is freely equal to pk, it is then the algorithm stops since g P H. □
The following result, due to Miha˘ılova [Mih58, Mih66], shows that
F2 ˆ F2 does not in fact have solvable subgroup membership problem.

Theorem I.B.1.5 (Miha˘ılova). The group F2 ˆ F2 has unsolvable sub-
group membership problem.
Proof. Note that F 2

define ∆ “ π´1pDq ă F 2

decide if px, yq P ∆.
It remains to check that ∆ is finitely generated. Let R be a finite set of
relations for Q. We claim that

is a generating set. The issue is that R only normally generates the kernel
of the map Fn ↠ Q, however, if w P Fn and r P R we have

of which each term is in S. □
F2xF2_notLERF
Corollary I.B.1.6. F2 ˆ F2 has inseparable subgroups.
I.B.2. LERF groups. We say a group G is extended residually finite
(ERF) if every subgroup of G is separable. We say G is locally extended
residually finite (LERF) if every finitely generated subgroup of G is separa-
ble.
Two groups G and H are commensurable if there exists finite index sub-
groups K ď G and L ď H such that K – L.
Exercise I.B.2.1. Let G be a finitely generated LERF group. If H is a
group commensurable with G, then H is LERF.
Examples I.B.2.2. We record a few examples:
(1) finite groups are trivially LERF;
(2) Z is also trivially seen to be LERF;
(3) Zn is LERF.
Proof. Let L ď Zn and let g P ZnzL. We may assume L has infinite
index since all finite index subgroups are separable by definition.
Since L Ÿ Zn we may consider the quotient πL : Zn ↠ A “ Zn{L.
Note that πLpgq ‰ 0. Now, A is a finitely generated abelian group
and hence residually finite. Thus, there exists a finite (abelian)
quotient α : A ↠ Q where αpπLpgqq ‰ 0 P Q. Moreover, kerpα ˝ πLq
contains L. Hence, we have separated L from g in Q. ˛
(4) It follows from Corollary I.B.1.6 that F2 ˆ F2 is not LERF.
A particularly famous result due to Marshall Hall Jr. is that free groups
are LERF [Hal49]. We divert the proof to the next section.
thm.MarshallHall
Theorem I.B.2.3 (Marshall Hall Jr.’s Theorem). Free groups are LERF
It follows from Theorem I.B.2.3 and Corollary I.B.1.6 that direct prod-
ucts of LERF groups are not necessarily LERF.
I.B. SUBGROUP SEPARABILITY 10
I.B.3. The connection with covering theory. Separability of sub-
groups has a particularly powerful interpretation when viewed from a topo-
logical lens. The following somewhat miraculous result of Scott allows one
to promote immersions to embeddings, at the cost of taking a finite cover,
when in the presence of subgroup separability.
Let X and Y be CW complexes. Recall that an immersion is map
f : X í Y such that f is a locally injective combinatorial map. The map f
map is combinatorial if it maps the interior of each cell homeomorphically
onto its image.
Theorem I.B.3.1. [Sco78]thm.Scott_LERF_geometric
Let G be a finitely generated group and let
X be a CW complex with π1X “ G. Let H ď G and let Y Ñ X be the
cover corresponding to H. Then, H is separable if and only if for every
finite subcomplex K Ď Y , there exists an intermediate finite sheeted cover
Y Ñ Z Ñ X such that K embeds as a subcomplex of Z.
Proof. Suppose the geometric condition holds for some subgroup H
with cover Y Ñ X and let g P GzH. Pick a basepoint x P rX in the
universal cover of X and let K “ πpx Y gxq Ď Y , where π : rX Ñ Y is the
universal covering map. By hypothesis we obtain a finite covering Z Ñ X
with corresponding finite index subgroup G1 such that K Ď Z. But, then
g R G1. Hence, H is separable.
Now, suppose H is a separable subgroup of G with corresponding cover
Y Ñ X and let K be a finite subcomplex of Y . Let π : rX Ñ Y be the
universal cover and let C “ π´1K. Pick a finite subcomplex D of C such
that πpDq “ K and note that S :“ tg P G : gD X D ‰ Hu is finite. Since H
is separable we can find a finite index subgroup G1 of G such that H ď G
and G1 X S Ď H. The cover rX{G1 Ñ X is then the required intermediate
finite cover. □
We will also need the following easy lemma. Graphs_imm_lifts
Lemma I.B.3.2. Let ∆ and Γ be finite graphs and let f : ∆ í Γ be an
immersion. Then extends f to a finite-sheeted covering ˆΓ Ñ Γ such that ∆
embeds in ˆΓ.
Proof. Fix an orientation and a colouring on the edges of Γ. This lifts
to an orientation and a colouring on ∆. A combinatorial map is an immersion
if and only if at each vertex, we see each colour arriving exactly once and
leaving exactly once. Let n denote the number of vertices of ∆ and for each
colour c of Γ let nc be the number of edges of ∆ coloured c. There are n ´ nc
vertices of ∆ missing incoming edges with colour c and the same number of
vertices missing outgoing edges with colour c. Pick any bijection between
these sets and glue in n ´ nc edges coloured c. Repeating this for each colour
we eventually obtain a covering space ˆΓ of Γ such that ∆ Ă ˆΓ. □
We now give Stalling’s proof of the Marshall Hall Theorem.
Proof of Theorem I.B.2.3. Now, let Γ be a finite rose and H a
finitely generated subgroup of F “ π1Γ. Let ∆ Ñ Γ be the covering cor-
responding to H, and consider a finite subcomplex K Ă ∆. Since H is
finitely generated, we may enlarge K to ensure that K is connected and
I.C. PRO TOPOLOGIES ON GROUPS 11
that π1K “ H. But ∆ Ñ Γ is an immersion, so can be completed to a
finite-sheeted covering ˆΓ Ñ Γ by Lemma I.B.3.2. The result now follows
from Theorem I.B.3.1. □
I.C. Pro topologies on groups
I.C.1. The definition.
Definition I.C.1.1 (Profinite topology). Let N be a non-empty collection of
finite index normal subgroups of a group G. We say N is filtered from below if
whenever N1, N2 P N , there exists N P N such that N ď N1 X N2. We make
G into a topological group by taking N as a basis of open neighbourhoods
of the identity the collection. We refer to the corresponding topology as a
pro topology on G.
Let H1, . . . , Hn be groups. A subdirect product of H “ śn
i“1 Hi is any
subgroup G of H such that the projections πi : G Ñ Hi are surjective.
Definition I.C.1.2 (Formation). A class of finite groups C is called a for-
mation if C is closed under taking quotients and subdirect products.
Examples I.C.1.3. The following are examples of formations:
(1) the class of all finite groups;
(2) the class of all finite abelian groups;
(3) the class of all finite nilpotent groups;
(4) the class of all finite soluble groups;
(5) the class of all finite p-groups for a fixed prime p.
Definition I.C.1.4 (Pro-C topology). Let C be a formation of finite groups.
The (full) pro-C topology on G is the topology τC pGq on G given by taking
as a basis of open neighbourhoods of the identity the collection
NC pGq :“ tN Ĳ G | G{N P Cu.
When C is the class of all finite groups, we denote the topology by τG and
refer to it as the profinite topology on G.
Lemma I.C.1.5. Let C be a formation of finite groups. A group G is resid-
ually C if and only if its pro-C topology is Hausdorff.
Proof. Suppose G is residually-C. Let g ‰ h be elements of G. Since,
gh´1 is non-trivial, there exists a finite index subgroup N ă G such that
gh´1 R N . Hence, gN X hN “ H. This proves the topology is Hausdorff.
Reversing the argument yields the converse. □
Definition I.C.1.6 (Separable subset). Let S be subset of G. We say S is
C-separable if S is closed in pro-C topology on G.
Lemma I.C.1.7. Let C be a formation of finite groups and let G be a group.
Suppose H ď G. Then, H is closed in the pro-C topology on G if and only
if H is the intersection of open subgroups of G.
Proof. An open subgroup in the topology τC pGq has finite index so it is
also a closed subgroup. Hence, any intersection of open subgroups is closed.
For the converse suppose H is closed in τC pGq and let g P GzH. There
exists some N P NC pGq such that xN X H “ H. Thus, g R HN . It follows
that H “ Ş
N PNC pGq HN . But each HN is open, whence the lemma. □
I.C. PRO TOPOLOGIES ON GROUPS 12
Exercise I.C.1.8. Let C be a formation of finite groups and let G be a
group. Suppose H is a finite index subgroup of G. Then, H is open in the
pro-C topology on G if and only if G{ CoreGpHq is in C.
In general the subspace topology induced on a subgroup H ď G from
the pro-C topology on G will not be equal to the pro-C topology on H but
instead some mystery pro topology. When the topology induced on H is
the pro-C topology we say that G induces the full pro-C topology on H. A
sufficient condition for this to happen is that every finite index subgroup of
H is separable in G.
Example I.C.1.9. If G is residually finite and H is a finite index subgroup
of G, then the inclusion H Ñ G is a homemorphism onto its image. That
is, G induces the full
I.C.2. Virtual retracts.
Definition I.C.2.1 (Virtual retract). Recall that H ď G is a retract if
the inclusion i : H ↣ G has a left inverse r : G ↠ H, that is r ˝ i “ idH .
Similarly, we call H a virtual retract, written H ďvr G, if H is a retract of a
finite index subgroup of G.
The following useful lemma is due to Hsu and Wise [HW99, Lemma 3.9].
lem.RetractsClosed
Lemma I.C.2.2. Let G be a residually finite group and let H ď G. If
r : G ↠ H is retract, then
(1) H is closed in the profinite topology on G;
(2) if K is closed in profinite topology on H, then K is closed in the
profinite topology on G;
(3) the inclusion map H Ñ G induces a homemorphism onto its image
of profinite topologies.
Proof. We first prove (1). Let N “ ker r and note that G “ N H and
N X H “ 1 so we may express any element of G uniquely as a product nh
with n P N and h P H. Since G is residually finite we may pick a chain pGiq
of finite index normal subgroups of G such that Ş
i Gi “ 1. Let Ni “ Gi X N
and note that |G : NiH| “ |N H : NiH| “ |N : Ni| ď |G : Gi|. Hence, pNiHq
is a sequence of finite index subgroups of H whose intersection is exactly H.
We now prove (2). Let K be a closed subgroup of H. Since r : G Ñ H
is continuous with respect to the profinite topologies, the preimage r´1pKq
is closed in G. Now, K “ H X r´1pKq and so is the intersection of closed
subgroups and hence closed in G.
The claim (3) follows from (2). □
lem.virRetractsClosed
Lemma I.C.2.3. Let G be a finitely generated residually finite group and
let H ď G. If H ďvr G, then H is closed in the profinite topology on G.
Proof. Let K ď G be a finite index subgroup admitting a retract
r : K ↠ H. By Lemma I.C.2.2 we see that H is closed in the profinite
topology on K. But K is finite index in G, so every closed subset of K is
closed in G. Hence, H ic closed in the profinite topology on G. □
We state some elementary properties of virtual retracts first collected by
Minasyan [Min21, Lemma 3.2].
I.C. PRO TOPOLOGIES ON GROUPS 13
Exercise I.C.2.4. Suppose that G and G1 are groups.
(1) Let H ďvr G and A ď G such that H ď A. Then, H ďvr A.
(2) Suppose H ď G and that there exists a homomorphism ϕ : G Ñ G1
such that ϕ|H is injective. If ϕpHq ďvr G1, then H ďvr G.
(3) If H ďvr G and α P AutpGq, then αpHq ďvr G.
(4) If H ďvr G and A ďvr H, then A ďvr G.
(5) If H ďvr G and H1 ďvr G1, then H ˆ H1 ďvr G ˆ G1.
(6) If G is finitely generated and H ďvr G, then H is undistorted in G.
Let G be finitely generated by a set S and H ď G be finitely generated by
a set T . We say H is undistorted Undistorted subgroup
if there exists a C ą 0 such that for all
h P H we have |h|T ď C ¨ |h|S . Where | ¨ |S and | ¨ |T are the word metrics in
G and H with respect to T and S respectively.
CHAPTER II
Profinite groups and completions
II.A. Profinite groups
II.A.1. Inverse limits and profinite spaces.
Definition II.A.1.1 (Directed poset). A pair I “ pI, ďq where I is a set
and ď is a binary relation is a directed poset if it is a poset:
(1) i ď i, for i P I;
(2) if i ď and j ď k, then i ď k, for i, j, k P I;
(3) if i ď j and j ď i, then i “ j, for i, j P I;
satisfying one additional condition
(4) if i, j P I, then there exists some k P I such that i, j ď k.
Definition II.A.1.2 (Inverse system). An inverse system of objects in a
category C over a directed poset I consists of a collection tXi | i P Iu of
objects in C indexed by I and a collection of morphisms φi,j : Xi Ñ Xj for
each j ď i such that
(1) if i ď j ď k we have φi,j ˝ φj,k “ φi,k, that is the following diagram
commutes
Xi Xj
Xk;
φi,j
φi,k φj,k
(2) and φi,i “ idXi .
Let Y be an object in some category C and let tXi, φi,j , Iu be an inverse
system of objects in C over a directed poset I.
Definition II.A.1.3 (Inverse limit). We say a sequence of morphisms ψi : Y Ñ
Xi is compatible (with I) if φi,j ˝ ψi “ ψj for j ď i.
Given an object X in C with compatible morphisms νi : X Ñ Xi, we say
X is an inverse limit of the inverse system tXi, φi,j , Iu if the following uni-
versal property holds: whenever Y is an object in C with a set of compatible
morphisms ψi : Y Ñ Xi, then there exists a unique morphism ψ : Y Ñ X
such that following diagram commutes
Y X
Xi
D!ψ
ψi νi
for every i P I. We will denote an inverse limit X by limÐÝ Xi.

Exercise II.A.1.4. Let I “ tXi, φi,j , Iu be an inverse system of objects in
a category C over a directed poset I. If an inverse limit of I exists, then it
is unique up to C-isomorphism.
Proposition II.A.1.5. Let I “ tXi, φi,j , Iu be an inverse system of topo-
logical spaces (or topological groups) over a directed poset I. Then, there
exists an inverse limit of I.
